teachings.teaching: One should not be attached to family and wealth; such attachment leads to bondage. Instead, one should fix the mind on the Supreme at the time of death.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Emphasizes the practice of constant remembrance (smriti) and detachment (vairagya) as essential for liberation.

teachings.teaching: Human birth in Bharata is supremely valuable because it offers the opportunity for devotion to Mukunda, which even gods envy.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This highlights the rarity and preciousness of human life for spiritual practice; the body is the instrument for yoga.

teachings.teaching: Those who perform sacrifices with faith but without seeking liberation remain in the cycle of birth and death.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Action (karma) without the goal of self-realization leads to further bondage; the true purpose of action is to purify the mind.

teachings.teaching: The Lord grants the desires of his devotees but ultimately bestows his own lotus feet, which ends all desires.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This illustrates the progression from karma-kanda to bhakti; the ultimate gift is the cessation of all wants.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the seven concentric islands (dvīpas) and their surrounding oceans, each ruled by a son of Priyavrata. It details the geography, mountains, rivers, inhabitants, and their modes of worship of the Supreme Lord in various forms. The chapter culminates with the Lokāloka mountain and the abode of the Supreme Lord as the inner controller.

story.title: The Islands of Bhū-maṇḍala and the Abode of the Lord
story.arc: Cosmological description of the seven dvīpas and the supreme abode beyond Lokāloka.
story.timeline_note: Cosmic time; the description is of the eternal structure of the universe.
story.comic_potential: The vast concentric islands and oceans, each with unique features and worship, could be visualized as a cosmic map with increasing grandeur.

teachings.teaching: The Supreme Lord is worshipped in various forms corresponding to the elements and deities, such as Soma, Agni, Varuṇa, Vāyu, and Brahma, according to the nature of the inhabitants.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of ishta-devata: the practitioner can approach the same Supreme through different forms suited to their temperament and stage of practice.

teachings.teaching: The Lord as the inner controller (antaryāmin) resides on Lokāloka mountain, surrounded by his associates, maintaining the welfare of all worlds.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes the inner presence of the Lord as the witness and guide; this verse describes that inner abode as the source of all spiritual power and protection.

teachings.teaching: The sun (Sūrya) is the source of all life and the eye of the Lord, dividing time and space.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the sun symbolizes the inner light of consciousness that illumines all experiences; meditation on the sun as the self is a key practice.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the celestial geography and the motion of the sun, moon, planets, and stars around Mount Meru and Dhruva (the polestar). It details the sun's chariot, the divisions of time (days, nights, months, seasons, and years), the orbits of the planets, and the arrangement of the nakṣatras. The chapter concludes with the description of the śiśumāra (dolphin) form of Viṣṇu, which encompasses all celestial bodies, and a meditation on that form.

story.title: The Celestial Order and the Sun's Chariot
story.arc: Cosmological description of the universe's structure and time cycles
story.timeline_note: Timeless; describes eternal cosmic cycles
story.comic_potential: Visualizing the sun's chariot with horses named after Vedic meters, the dolphin-shaped constellation of Viṣṇu, and the planets orbiting Meru could be depicted in a grand cosmic comic.

teachings.teaching: The sun god Sūrya is the self of all, and by his movements he creates day and night, seasons, and years. He is to be worshipped through Vedic rituals and yoga.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the sun's course represents the inner journey of prāṇa through the suṣumṇā, with the solstices corresponding to the idā and piṅgalā channels.

teachings.teaching: The moon is the source of nourishment for all beings, and its waxing and waning determine the days of the gods and ancestors.
teachings.lens_note: The moon's phases mirror the fluctuations of prāṇa and mind; the bright fortnight represents upward movement (udāna), the dark fortnight downward (apāna).

teachings.teaching: The planets and stars are fixed on the wheel of time, revolving around Dhruva, who is the pivot established by Viṣṇu.
teachings.lens_note: Dhruva symbolizes the steady point of consciousness (sākṣī) around which all mental modifications revolve.

teachings.teaching: The śiśumāra form of Viṣṇu is a meditation device; by contemplating it, one attains liberation.
teachings.lens_note: This cosmic dolphin represents the kundalinī energy coiled at the base; meditating on its parts awakens the chakras.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the celestial arrangement of planets, stars, and constellations as the presiding deities of the universe. It states that worshiping or remembering them at the three junctures of the day (morning, noon, evening) destroys sins incurred during those times.

story.title: The Celestial Deities and Sin Removal
story.arc: Description of the cosmic order and the efficacy of worshiping celestial bodies.
story.timeline_note: Timeless description of the cosmic arrangement.
story.comic_potential: Low; purely descriptive and ritualistic.

teachings.teaching: Worshiping or remembering the presiding deities of planets, stars, and constellations at the three junctures of the day destroys sins committed during those times.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the lower planetary systems (bila-svarga) beneath the earth, including the seven subterranean realms (atala, vitala, sutala, talātala, mahātala, rasātala, pātāla), their inhabitants (daityas, dānavas, nāgas, etc.), and the glories of Lord Ananta (Sesa). It also details the hellish planets (naraka) where sinners suffer according to their deeds, and concludes with the description of the universal form of the Lord.

story.title: Description of the Subterranean Realms and Hellish Planets
story.arc: Cosmology and Karma
story.timeline_note: Cosmic time; eternal realms
story.comic_potential: Vivid descriptions of hellish tortures and opulent subterranean cities

teachings.teaching: The Lord's Sudarśana cakra protects the universe from Rāhu.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the Sudarśana cakra represents the divine will that cuts through ignorance.

teachings.teaching: Bali Mahārāja attained great opulence by giving everything to the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes surrender (prapatti) as the highest path; Bali exemplifies total self-offering.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's name, even uttered accidentally, destroys sins.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, nāma-japa is a powerful practice to purify the mind and attain liberation.

teachings.teaching: Meditation on Ananta (Śeṣa) cuts the knot of ignorance in the heart.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that dhyāna on the cosmic serpent represents the awakening of kuṇḍalinī and dissolution of ego.

teachings.teaching: Sinners suffer in various hells according to their deeds.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga stresses that karma must be exhausted; hells are temporary purgatorial states.

teachings.teaching: The universal form of the Lord (virāṭ-rūpa) is described as the sum of all creation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees the virāṭ as the macrocosmic body, which the yogi realizes within.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the process of meditation on the gross and subtle forms of the Lord, guiding the yogi to withdraw the mind from the gross to the subtle, culminating in realization of the Supreme.

story.title: Meditation on the Gross and Subtle Forms
story.arc: Instruction on yogic meditation
story.timeline_note: Timeless teaching
story.comic_potential: A yogi's inner journey from gross to subtle, visualized as a cosmic map.

teachings.teaching: Having conquered the mind on the gross form, one should gradually lead it to the subtle form through intelligence.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the transition from external practices (asana, pranayama) to internal meditative absorption (dhyana), where the mind is refined from gross objects to subtle energies.

teachings.teaching: The gross form of the Lord is the abode of all living beings, encompassing the entire cosmic manifestation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the gross form is not separate from the subtle; it is the same reality perceived at different levels. The yogi must realize the unity of the macrocosm and microcosm.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about the path of nivṛtti (renunciation) and pravṛtti (worldly activity), and how one can avoid hellish punishments. Śuka explains that mere prāyaścitta (atonement) is insufficient; true purification comes through devotion to Vāsudeva (Kṛṣṇa). He narrates the story of Ajāmila, a fallen brāhmaṇa who, at death, called out his son's name 'Nārāyaṇa' and was saved by Viṣṇudūtas from the messengers of Yamarāja. The Viṣṇudūtas debate with the Yamadūtas about the nature of dharma and adharma, emphasizing that sincere chanting of the Lord's name absolves all sins.

story.title: The Deliverance of Ajāmila
story.arc: Parīkṣit's inquiry leads to Śuka's discourse on the power of the Lord's name, illustrated by Ajāmila's story.
story.timeline_note: The story of Ajāmila is a past event narrated by Śuka to Parīkṣit.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic confrontation between the divine messengers and the personified forces of justice, with Ajāmila's last-minute redemption, has strong visual and emotional impact.

teachings.teaching: Mere atonement (prāyaścitta) is insufficient to cleanse sins; one must turn towards the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that external rituals without inner transformation are ineffective. True purification comes from constant remembrance and service to the Divine.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Vāsudeva (Kṛṣṇa) burns away all sins like the sun dispels fog.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this refers to the power of bhakti (devotion) as a direct means to transcend karmic bonds, surpassing mere ascetic practices.

teachings.teaching: Even a single utterance of the Lord's name at the time of death can save one from hell.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma highlights that the name of God carries intrinsic spiritual potency; when uttered with sincerity, it connects the soul to the divine, overriding past misdeeds.

teachings.teaching: Dharma is defined by the Vedas, which are identical to Nārāyaṇa; adharma is the opposite.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's view that the scriptures are not mere texts but manifestations of the divine will, guiding one towards harmony with cosmic order.

teachings.teaching: The soul is bound by the three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) and their resultant actions; liberation comes through association with the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that by transcending the guṇas through disciplined practice and devotion, one attains freedom from the cycle of karma.

chapter_summary: The chapter narrates the story of Ajamila, a fallen brahmana who, at the moment of death, calls out to his son Narayana, inadvertently uttering the holy name of Lord Vishnu. The Vishnudutas (messengers of Vishnu) rescue him from the Yamadutas (messengers of Yama), arguing that even an unintentional utterance of the Lord's name absolves all sins. Ajamila, freed, repents and goes to the Ganges to practice yoga, ultimately attaining liberation and reaching Vishnu's abode.

story.title: The Deliverance of Ajamila
story.arc: Ajamila, a sinful brahmana, is saved from hell by uttering the name of Narayana at death, leading to his repentance and ultimate liberation.
story.timeline_note: The story is narrated by Shuka to Parikshit as an illustration of the power of the Lord's name.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic confrontation between divine messengers, a near-death experience, and a transformative redemption arc.

teachings.teaching: Even unintentional utterance of the Lord's name (like 'Narayana') can absolve all sins and grant liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the power of nama-japa (chanting the divine name) as a spontaneous kriya that purifies the mind and connects one to the divine, regardless of intention.

teachings.teaching: The name of the Lord is more powerful than any prescribed atonement (prayashchitta) for removing sins.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would highlight that kriya-yoga, especially nama-sankirtana, directly purifies the heart, whereas ritual atonements may not address the root cause of desire.

teachings.teaching: One should abandon attachment to the body and material relationships, and fix the mind on the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga practice of pratyahara (withdrawal of senses) and dharana (concentration) to transcend the false ego.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's name is a boat that saves even the most fallen from the ocean of material existence.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the name is a powerful mantra that awakens the dormant spiritual energy (kundalini) and leads to self-realization.

chapter_summary: Yama explains to his messengers that the supreme authority is Lord Vishnu, not himself. He describes the power of chanting the Lord's names, citing Ajamila's liberation. Yama lists the twelve authorities on Bhagavata Dharma and instructs his servants to avoid those who are devoted to Krishna.

story.title: Yama Instructs His Messengers
story.arc: Yama explains the supremacy of Vishnu and the power of His holy name, listing the twelve authorities on Bhagavata Dharma.
story.timeline_note: After Ajamila's liberation, Yama's messengers report to him, and Yama responds.
story.comic_potential: Yama's messengers being humbled by Vishnu's servants and Yama's subsequent praise of the Lord's name.

teachings.teaching: The ultimate controller is Lord Vishnu, not Yama or any other deity.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes surrendering to the inner Self (Vishnu) rather than external authorities.

teachings.teaching: Chanting the Lord's names, even unconsciously, can liberate one from death.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees nama-sankirtana as a direct method to purify the mind and attain liberation.

teachings.teaching: The twelve authorities on Bhagavata Dharma are the true knowers of dharma.
teachings.lens_note: These twelve represent the lineage of realized souls; in Kriya-Yoga, they are exemplars of devotion.

teachings.teaching: Devotees of Krishna are beyond the jurisdiction of Yama's punishment.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that one who is established in the Self is free from karmic reactions.

teachings.teaching: The holy name of the Lord is the supreme atonement for sins.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, nama-japa is a powerful purifier that erases past karmas.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about the secondary creation (anusarga) by Dakṣa. Śuka narrates how the ten Prācetasa sons, emerging from the ocean, became angry at the trees covering the earth and emitted fire from their mouths. King Soma (the Moon god) pacified them, advising them to control anger and accept a maiden (Māriṣā) as wife. They married her, and from her was born Dakṣa Prācetasa. Dakṣa then performed penance at Aghamarṣaṇa tīrtha, praised the Lord with the Haṃsa-guhya hymn, and was granted a wife (Asiknī) and the power to create progeny through sexual union.

story.title: The Birth of Dakṣa and His Penance
story.arc: Secondary creation (anusarga) by Dakṣa
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the primary creation by Brahmā, in the Svāyambhuva manvantara.
story.comic_potential: The Prācetasas' anger at trees and Soma's intervention could be depicted humorously, with the trees personified and the sons' fiery breath.

teachings.teaching: Control anger; do not harm the helpless.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, anger is a klesha that disturbs the mind; controlling it is essential for inner peace and spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The Lord resides in all beings as the inner Self.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga teaching of seeing the divine in all, fostering compassion and non-violence.

teachings.teaching: One who restrains anger for self-realization transcends the guṇas.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that controlling emotions is a key practice in Kriya-Yoga to attain higher consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the source of all creation and the ultimate object of worship.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the devotional aspect of Kriya-Yoga, where surrender to the divine is central.

teachings.teaching: Penance and devotion please the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga incorporates tapas (austerity) and bhakti (devotion) as means to purify the mind and attain grace.

chapter_summary: Daksha, at Brahma's behest, first produces ten thousand Haryashva sons who are sent to create progeny. They encounter Narada, who dissuades them with riddles about the nature of the world, leading them to renounce and take the path of no return. Daksha then produces another thousand Savalashva sons, who also meet Narada and are similarly led away. Daksha curses Narada, but Narada accepts it calmly.

story.title: The Dissuasion of Daksha's Sons by Narada
story.arc: Daksha's repeated attempts to create progeny are thwarted by Narada's teachings on renunciation.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Daksha's previous creation of progeny; sequential events within the same cosmic period.
story.comic_potential: Narada's riddles and the sons' naive acceptance create a humorous contrast with Daksha's frustration.

teachings.teaching: Without understanding the true nature of the self and the world, actions (karma) are futile.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that external actions without inner realization (kriya) are fruitless; true yoga involves turning inward.

teachings.teaching: One should seek the supreme self (Narayana) beyond the material world.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that the goal is to realize the indwelling self (Atman) as identical with Brahman, achieved through meditation and mantra repetition.

teachings.teaching: A sage endures curses and insults with equanimity, seeing them as the will of the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of non-reaction (samatva) and surrender to the divine will.

chapter_summary: This chapter enumerates the progeny of Daksha's daughters, detailing the lineages of various deities, sages, demons, and other beings. It traces the descendants of Dharma, Bhuta, Angiras, Kṛśāśva, Tārkṣya, Soma, and Kaśyapa, including the Ādityas, Vasus, Rudras, Maruts, and others. The chapter also describes the marriages and offspring of Kaśyapa's wives, leading to the creation of diverse species.

story.title: Chapter 6
story.arc: This chapter enumerates the progeny of Daksha's daughters, detailing the lineages of various deities, sages, demons, and other beings. It traces the descendants of Dharma, Bhuta, Angiras, Kṛśāśva, Tārkṣya, Soma, and Kaśyapa, including the Ādityas, Vasus, Rudras, Maruts, and others. The chapter also describes the marriages and offspring of Kaśyapa's wives, leading to the creation of diverse species.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka how the demons defeated the gods after Indra insulted his guru Bṛhaspati. Śuka narrates: Indra, intoxicated by power, failed to honor Bṛhaspati when he arrived. Bṛhaspati left, and the demons, under Śukrācārya's guidance, attacked the gods. The gods sought refuge in Brahmā, who advised them to approach Viśvarūpa, son of Tvaṣṭā, as their priest. Viśvarūpa accepted and, through the Viṣṇu-vidyā, restored Indra's prosperity and defeated the demons.

story.title: Indra's Fall and Redemption through Viśvarūpa
story.arc: Indra's pride leads to loss of guru and defeat; through humility and Brahmā's guidance, he gains Viśvarūpa as priest and recovers prosperity.
story.timeline_note: After the events of the previous chapter (bhp_06.06), this chapter begins with Parīkṣit's question.
story.comic_potential: Indra's pompous court scene, his sudden realization of folly, and the demons' triumphant attack provide dramatic contrasts.

teachings.teaching: Pride and disrespect towards a guru lead to downfall, even for a king of gods.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates how ego (asmitā) disrupts the flow of grace (guru-kṛpā). Indra's intoxication with power (aiśvarya-mada) blinds him to the guru's presence, a key obstacle in spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: True repentance and humility can restore lost grace.
teachings.lens_note: Indra's self-criticism (garhayāmāsa) is a form of pratyāhāra (turning inward). In Kriya-Yoga, this introspective remorse is the first step toward purification.

teachings.teaching: The guru's departure is a consequence of disrespect, but the disciple can seek remedy through higher guidance.
teachings.lens_note: Brahmā represents the higher Self (ātman) or the cosmic intelligence that guides when the external guru is absent. In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to turning to the inner guru after outer mistakes.

teachings.teaching: Seeking a qualified brahmin (guru) is essential for overcoming adversaries.
teachings.lens_note: Viśvarūpa, though young, is chosen for his spiritual power (tapas). In Kriya-Yoga, the guru is not necessarily old but one who has realized the Self.

teachings.teaching: The power of divine knowledge (Viṣṇu-vidyā) can overcome even the strongest demonic forces.
teachings.lens_note: This vidyā is a form of mantra or meditation technique. In Kriya-Yoga, it corresponds to the specific practices that awaken inner power and protect the practitioner.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about the Nārāyaṇa-kavaca, a protective hymn taught by Viśvarūpa to Indra. The hymn invokes various avatars and weapons of Viṣṇu for protection from all dangers. Śuka concludes by describing its power and the story of the sage Kauśika who used it.

story.title: The Nārāyaṇa-kavaca: The Armor of Nārāyaṇa
story.arc: Teaching of the protective hymn by Viśvarūpa to Indra, and its efficacy illustrated by the story of Kauśika.
story.timeline_note: The hymn is taught in a past era; the story of Kauśika is also past relative to the narration.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic fall of Citraratha's vimana upon encountering the bones of Kauśika has visual humor.

teachings.teaching: The Nārāyaṇa-kavaca is a powerful protective hymn that invokes Viṣṇu's avatars and weapons for safety from all dangers.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this hymn represents the internal armor of yogic discipline, where each avatar corresponds to a specific energy center or practice that protects the practitioner from inner and outer obstacles.

teachings.teaching: Reciting the names and forms of Hari destroys all adversities.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the power of nāma-japa (chanting divine names) to purify the mind and align with the cosmic will, removing karmic impediments.

teachings.teaching: One who wears this armor is freed from fear of kings, thieves, ghosts, diseases, etc.
teachings.lens_note: This corresponds to the yogi's immunity to external disturbances through inner stability and connection to the divine.

chapter_summary: The gods, terrified by the demon Vṛtra (created by Tvaṣṭā), seek refuge in Viṣṇu. Viṣṇu instructs them to obtain the bones of the sage Dadhyañca, which will be used to make a weapon to kill Vṛtra. The chapter also recounts the origin of Vṛtra from Tvaṣṭā's sacrifice and the distribution of Brahmahatyā (sin of killing a brāhmaṇa) among earth, trees, women, and water.

story.title: The Origin of Vṛtra and the Gods' Plea to Viṣṇu
story.arc: The gods, oppressed by the demon Vṛtra, seek Viṣṇu's help. Viṣṇu instructs them to obtain Dadhyañca's bones to forge a weapon. The chapter sets up the eventual slaying of Vṛtra.
story.timeline_note: After Viśvarūpa's death, Tvaṣṭā creates Vṛtra. The gods immediately appeal to Viṣṇu, who then directs them to Dadhyañca.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic appearance of Vṛtra, the gods' panic, and Viṣṇu's calm instruction to seek bones from a sage offer vivid imagery.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the sole refuge; even gods must surrender to Him.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the necessity of surrendering the ego (ahamkara) to the divine will, aligning individual action with cosmic purpose.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's actions are beyond dualities; He creates, sustains, and destroys without being affected.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as the witness consciousness (sakshi) that remains untouched by the play of gunas, guiding the practitioner to identify with the Self rather than the doer.

teachings.teaching: Devotion and remembrance of the Lord lead to liberation from samsara.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on constant remembrance (smriti) and devotion (bhakti) as means to still the mind and realize the Self.

teachings.teaching: The wise do not grant harmful desires; they give what is truly beneficial.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the guru (or inner Self) guides the practitioner away from fleeting pleasures toward lasting spiritual good, mirroring the principle of viveka (discrimination).

chapter_summary: The chapter describes the battle between the devas and asuras, focusing on the sage Dadhyan (Dadhyaṅṅ Ātharvaṇa) who sacrifices his body for the devas, enabling Indra to obtain the vajra weapon. The asuras, led by Vṛtra, attack fiercely but are repelled by the devas. Vṛtra then delivers a speech on the inevitability of death and the glory of dying in battle.

story.title: The Battle Between Devas and Asuras: Dadhyaṅṅ's Sacrifice and Vṛtra's Speech
story.arc: After the devas request Dadhyaṅṅ to give his body, he meditates and offers it to the Supreme. Indra, armed with the vajra made from Dadhyaṅṅ's bones, leads the devas against the asuras. The asuras, led by Vṛtra, attack fiercely but are repelled. Vṛtra then rebukes his fleeing followers, extolling the glory of dying in battle.
story.timeline_note: The battle occurs at the beginning of the Tretā Yuga on the banks of the Narmadā River.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic sacrifice of Dadhyaṅṅ, the forging of the vajra, the intense battle with various weapons, and Vṛtra's rousing speech offer vivid visual and narrative elements.

teachings.teaching: The body is impermanent; one should not be attached to it but use it for higher purposes like dharma and compassion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes detachment from the physical body and using it as an instrument for spiritual practice and service.

teachings.teaching: True dharma is to feel the joy and sorrow of others as one's own.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on empathy and selfless action as a means to purify the mind.

teachings.teaching: Death is certain for the born; one should embrace a noble death in battle for righteousness rather than flee.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees facing death with courage and duty as a form of yoga, surrendering the ego.

chapter_summary: Vritra, the demon king, rallies his fleeing army, rebukes them for cowardice, and single-handedly attacks the gods. He defeats Indra's elephant Airavata and then delivers a profound discourse on the futility of material desires and the supreme value of devotion to Vishnu, before urging Indra to strike him with the thunderbolt.

story.title: Vritra's Discourse and Death
story.arc: Vritra, after rallying his troops, engages Indra in battle, defeats Airavata, and then delivers a philosophical speech on renunciation and devotion, ultimately urging Indra to kill him.
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs during the war between the devas and asuras, after Vritra's brother was killed by Indra.
story.comic_potential: High: Vritra's dramatic rebuke of his army, his single-handed assault on the gods, and his philosophical monologue while facing death offer strong visual and emotional contrasts.

teachings.teaching: Material achievements (heaven, sovereignty, yogic powers) are worthless without devotion to the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that all external accomplishments are distractions; true yoga is inner surrender to the divine.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's grace is often hidden in adversity, which removes attachment to worldly pursuits.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that obstacles are divine arrangements to purify the mind and turn it inward.

teachings.teaching: The devotee desires only to be a servant of the Lord's servant, with mind, speech, and body engaged in remembering and glorifying Him.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on constant remembrance (smaraṇa) and action dedicated to the divine.

teachings.teaching: The soul longs for the Lord like a wingless bird for its mother, a calf for milk, or a separated lover for the beloved.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the natural pull of the soul toward its source, which Kriya-Yoga cultivates through meditation.

chapter_summary: Vritra, the demon king, fights Indra. Despite losing an arm, Vritra preaches about the supremacy of the Supreme Lord and the futility of ego. Indra, inspired, fights on and eventually kills Vritra, who attains liberation.

story.title: The Battle of Indra and Vritra
story.arc: Vritra's defiance and eventual liberation through devotion
story.timeline_note: After Vritra's arm is cut off, he preaches; then Indra kills him.
story.comic_potential: Vritra swallowing Indra whole, then Indra cutting his way out.

teachings.teaching: The Supreme Lord is the sole controller; all beings act under His will.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes surrendering the ego to the divine will, recognizing that all actions are ultimately guided by the Supreme.

teachings.teaching: One should remain equipoised in victory and defeat, pleasure and pain.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's practice of equanimity through inner stillness, transcending dualities.

teachings.teaching: The soul is the witness of the three modes of nature and is not bound by them.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that by realizing the self as the witness (sākṣī), one attains liberation.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to the Lord is the highest goal, surpassing all material achievements.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga sees bhakti as the culmination of practice, leading to immersion in the divine.

chapter_summary: After Indra kills Vritra, he is afflicted by the sin of brahmahatya (killing a brahmana). The sages advise him to perform an Ashvamedha sacrifice to the Supreme Lord Narayana to purify himself. Indra flees from the sin personified as a hideous woman, hides in a lotus stalk for a thousand years, and during his absence, Nahusha rules heaven but falls due to pride. Eventually, the sages perform the horse sacrifice, and Indra is purified and restored.

story.title: Indra's Fall and Restoration
story.arc: After killing Vritra, Indra is burdened by brahmahatya. He flees, hides in a lotus stalk, and during his absence Nahusha rules heaven but falls. The sages perform an Ashvamedha for Indra, who is purified and restored.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Vritra's death; Indra hides for a thousand years; then Nahusha's rule; then Indra's restoration.
story.comic_potential: The personified sin chasing Indra, his hiding in a lotus stalk, and Nahusha's comical fall due to kicking Indra's wife.

teachings.teaching: Even the greatest sin can be purified by performing a sacrifice to the Supreme Lord with faith.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that kriya (action) dedicated to the divine purifies all karmic residues.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's name and glories have the power to cleanse all sins.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework highlights the transformative power of divine name (nāma-japa) as a core kriya.

teachings.teaching: Pride in material opulence leads to downfall.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's yoga warns against the ego's inflation from siddhis; humility is essential.

teachings.teaching: The Supreme Lord is the ultimate purifier and the goal of all sacrifices.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees all practices as leading to union with the inner Self, the Puruṣa.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka how the demon Vṛtra, born of rajas and tamas, could have such firm devotion to Nārāyaṇa. Śuka replies by narrating the story of King Citraketu, who was childless despite many wives. The sage Aṅgirā visits him, and Citraketu expresses his sorrow. Aṅgirā performs a sacrifice and gives the remnants to Citraketu's chief queen Kṛtadyuti, who conceives a son. The other queens, jealous, poison the child. Citraketu and Kṛtadyuti lament bitterly. Aṅgirā and Nārada then arrive.

story.title: The Story of King Citraketu
story.arc: Parīkṣit's question about Vṛtra's devotion leads to the tale of Citraketu's grief and the arrival of sages.
story.timeline_note: Past event narrated by Śuka to Parīkṣit.
story.comic_potential: The lamentation of Citraketu and Kṛtadyuti over their dead son is highly emotional and dramatic.

teachings.teaching: Even those born of rajas and tamas can attain firm devotion to Nārāyaṇa.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that the gunas are not fixed; through practice and grace, one can transcend them.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Mukunda is rare even among pure sages.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that true devotion is a gift, not a product of effort alone; it requires surrender.

teachings.teaching: Among many, few seek liberation; among those, even fewer achieve it; among the liberated, rare is the devotee of Nārāyaṇa.
teachings.lens_note: This hierarchy underscores the rarity of bhakti, which in Kriya-Yoga is the ultimate goal beyond mere liberation.

teachings.teaching: Attachment to sense objects leads to suffering, as seen in Citraketu's grief.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would point out that the king's sorrow stems from identification with the body and relationships; Kriya-Yoga teaches detachment through inner practice.

chapter_summary: King Citraketu, grieving his son's death, is consoled by the sages Angira and Narada. They teach him about the illusory nature of the body and attachments, urging him to seek the Supreme Lord. Narada gives him a mantra to realize Sankarshana.

story.title: The Consolation of King Citraketu
story.arc: Citraketu's grief and enlightenment through the teachings of Angira and Narada
story.timeline_note: After the death of Citraketu's son
story.comic_potential: High; dramatic scene of grieving king visited by two sages in disguise, leading to a profound teaching and a mantra for realization.

teachings.teaching: The body and its relationships are temporary like sandbanks in a river; the self is eternal.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the need to detach from the transient physical and emotional states to realize the eternal Self.

teachings.teaching: The Lord creates, sustains, and destroys beings through His maya, yet remains unaffected like a child playing.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as the play of consciousness (Kriya) manifesting the universe without attachment.

teachings.teaching: The distinction between body and self is due to ignorance; in reality, all is one.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the non-dual realization in Kriya-Yoga where the practitioner sees the unity of all existence.

teachings.teaching: Wealth, family, and kingdom are like a city of Gandharvas, a dream, or a fantasy; they cause sorrow, delusion, and fear.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that worldly pursuits are distractions from the inner path of Kriya; one must withdraw from them.

teachings.teaching: The body, composed of matter, senses, and actions, is a source of various miseries.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the body is seen as a tool for spiritual practice, not an end in itself.

teachings.teaching: With a calm mind, reflect on the nature of the self and abandon attachment to duality; attain peace.
teachings.lens_note: This is the core of Kriya-Yoga: stilling the mind to realize the Self beyond dualities.

teachings.teaching: By meditating on the mantra given by Narada, one will see Sankarshana (the Lord) within seven days.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes mantra meditation as a direct means to realize the divine.

chapter_summary: Narada revives the dead son of King Citraketu and teaches him about the eternal nature of the soul. Citraketu, enlightened, performs penance, receives a mantra from Narada, and attains the vision of Lord Ananta (Sesha). He praises the Lord and receives further instruction on non-duality and devotion.

story.title: The Liberation of Citraketu
story.arc: Citraketu's journey from grief to enlightenment through the teachings of Narada and the grace of Lord Ananta.
story.timeline_note: After the death of Citraketu's son; Narada intervenes; Citraketu performs penance; attains vision of Ananta; receives instruction.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic revival of a dead prince, philosophical dialogue, penance, divine vision, and a powerful hymn of praise.

teachings.teaching: The soul is eternal and transmigrates through various bodies; relationships are temporary and based on karma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes the need to transcend identification with the body and realize the eternal Self through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: The Self is the witness of all mental states and is unaffected by the qualities of the mind.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the practitioner learns to observe thoughts without attachment, realizing the witness consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is both the material and efficient cause of the universe; the world is a manifestation of His energy.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary highlights that the entire cosmos is a projection of the divine, and the yogi perceives unity behind diversity.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to the Lord leads to liberation; even a fallen person can be freed by hearing His name.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga incorporates bhakti as a powerful means to purify the mind and attain self-realization.

teachings.teaching: The ultimate goal of human life is to realize the oneness of the individual self with the Supreme Self.
teachings.lens_note: This is the core of Kriya-Yoga: direct experience of the unity of Atman and Brahman through meditation.

chapter_summary: Citraketu, a Vidyadhara king, mocks Lord Shiva for publicly embracing his wife Parvati. Parvati curses him to become a demon. Citraketu accepts the curse with equanimity, teaching about the illusory nature of duality. Lord Shiva then explains to Parvati the greatness of Vishnu's devotees, who are beyond dualities. Citraketu is later born as the demon Vritra.

story.title: The Curse of Citraketu and the Glory of Vishnu's Devotees
story.arc: Citraketu mocks Shiva, is cursed by Parvati, accepts it gracefully, and is later born as Vritra; Shiva teaches Parvati about the transcendence of devotees.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Citraketu's previous life as a Vidyadhara king; his next birth as Vritra is foretold.
story.comic_potential: High: Citraketu's bold mockery of Shiva in a public assembly, Parvati's fiery curse, and Citraketu's calm acceptance create dramatic tension. Shiva's subsequent teaching provides a philosophical resolution.

teachings.teaching: The wise see all dualities (pleasure/pain, curse/blessing) as illusory; the self is not the doer.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the practice of witnessing the play of gunas without identification, leading to equanimity.

teachings.teaching: Devotees of Vasudeva are beyond fear and dualities; they see equally in heaven, liberation, or hell.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that such devotees have stabilized in the 'witness consciousness' (sakshi bhava), unaffected by external circumstances.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is equally disposed to all beings; He is the dearest of all.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga ideal of seeing the same Self in all, cultivating universal love.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the lineage of various beings from the creator deities, focusing on the progeny of Kashyapa and his wives, especially Diti. It describes the birth of the Maruts from Diti's womb, which was cut into seven pieces by Indra, but the children survived due to the grace of Vishnu. The story highlights Diti's vow to have a son to kill Indra, her observance of a year-long vow, Indra's attempt to find a flaw, and the eventual transformation of the Maruts into divine beings.

story.title: The Birth of the Maruts
story.arc: Genealogy and the story of Diti's vow and the Maruts' origin
story.timeline_note: The chapter begins with genealogical lists from earlier times, then focuses on the specific story of Diti and the Maruts, which occurs after the death of Diti's sons Hiraṇyakaśipu and Hiraṇyākṣa.
story.comic_potential: Indra's sneaky attempt to find a flaw in Diti's vow, his cutting of the fetus, and the miraculous survival of the Maruts have dramatic and humorous elements.

teachings.teaching: The husband is the supreme deity for a wife; serving him is equivalent to serving the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the practice of seeing the divine in the partner, transforming household life into a spiritual discipline.

teachings.teaching: Women's nature is fickle and self-serving; even a wise man can be deluded by a woman's charm.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a caution about the power of māyā (illusion) and the need for inner vigilance in Kriya-Yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: One should not harm any living being, speak falsehood, or engage in impure acts during a vow.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the yama (restraints) in Kriya-Yoga, emphasizing purity and non-violence as foundational.

teachings.teaching: Worship of the Lord, even for a short time, yields great results; the Maruts attained divinity despite Indra's attack.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would highlight the power of sincere devotion (bhakti) and the grace of the Lord, which can transform even adverse circumstances.

teachings.teaching: Those who worship the Lord without desire for rewards are truly wise; even the highest pleasures are insignificant compared to self-realization.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga ideal of nishkama karma (action without attachment) and the pursuit of inner liberation.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the Puṃsavana vow (vrata) dedicated to Lord Viṣṇu and Goddess Śrī, including its procedure, mantras, offerings, and the benefits for both men and women who observe it. The vow is performed over twelve months, culminating in a final ceremony. It emphasizes devotion, purity, and the grace of Viṣṇu and Śrī for fulfilling desires.

story.title: The Puṃsavana Vow for Viṣṇu and Śrī
story.arc: Instruction on the vow, its procedure, and its fruits.
story.timeline_note: The vow spans twelve months, with the final ceremony on the last day of Kārtika.
story.comic_potential: The detailed ritual instructions could be visualized as a step-by-step guide, with the couple performing the vow and receiving blessings.

teachings.teaching: The vow (vrata) is a means to please Lord Viṣṇu and Goddess Śrī, who are the supreme causes of the universe and bestowers of all desires.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the practice of disciplined action (kriya) performed with devotion, purifying the mind and attracting divine grace.

teachings.teaching: The vow involves daily worship, offerings, mantra recitation, and a final homa (fire sacrifice) with twelve oblations.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that such external rituals, when done with inner awareness and devotion, become a form of kriya-yoga that harmonizes the practitioner's energies.

teachings.teaching: Observing this vow brings various benefits: for men, fulfillment of desires; for women, beauty, wealth, children, a good husband, and removal of inauspiciousness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see these as natural outcomes of purified karma and divine grace, not as materialistic ends but as signs of spiritual progress.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (Book 7, Chapter 1) begins with King Yudhishthira asking Sage Narada how the Lord, who is impartial, could kill the demons for Indra's sake, and how enemies like Shishupala and Dantavakra attained liberation. Narada explains that the Lord, though transcendent, acts through the modes of nature, and that even hatred and fear can lead to liberation if the mind is fixed on Krishna. He recounts the story of Jaya and Vijaya, Vishnu's gatekeepers, who were cursed by the Sananda Kumaras to be born as demons, and their subsequent births as Hiranyakashipu, Hiranyaksha, Ravana, Kumbhakarna, Shishupala, and Dantavakra, all of whom were killed by the Lord's avatars and thus returned to Vaikuntha.

story.title: The Story of Jaya and Vijaya: Liberation Through Enmity
story.arc: Yudhishthira questions Narada about the Lord's impartiality and the liberation of enemies; Narada explains the Lord's actions through the modes of nature and narrates the curse of Jaya and Vijaya, their births as demons, and their eventual return to Vaikuntha through being killed by the Lord's avatars.
story.timeline_note: The conversation occurs after the Rajasuya sacrifice, where Shishupala was killed by Krishna. Narada recounts events from previous yugas (Satya, Treta, Dvapara) up to the present.
story.comic_potential: The irony of gatekeepers becoming demons and being killed by their own Lord, and the twist that hatred leads to liberation, offers dramatic and philosophical depth.

teachings.teaching: The Lord, though transcendent, acts through the modes of nature (sattva, rajas, tamas) to maintain cosmic order, favoring the demigods when sattva prevails.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of aligning with sattva (purity) through disciplined action, as the Lord's play of gunas guides the practitioner toward balance.

teachings.teaching: Even hatred or fear, if intensely focused on the Lord, can lead to liberation, as seen in the cases of Shishupala and Dantavakra.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that any intense mental absorption, even negative, can purify the mind if directed toward the divine, akin to the transformative power of sustained practice.

teachings.teaching: The mind should be fixed on Krishna by any means—love, hatred, fear, or kinship—as constant remembrance leads to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's core of continuous remembrance (smriti) and meditation on the divine, regardless of the initial emotional flavor.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's devotees, even when cursed, are ultimately protected and return to Him, as seen with Jaya and Vijaya.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as the unbreakable bond between the practitioner and the divine, where even apparent setbacks serve the soul's ultimate journey.

chapter_summary: Hiraṇyakaśipu, enraged by the death of his brother Hiraṇyākṣa at the hands of Viṣṇu (as the boar incarnation), orders the demons to devastate the earth, targeting brahmins, cows, and Vedic practices. The demons wreak havoc, causing the gods to flee. Hiraṇyakaśipu consoles his grieving family with a philosophical discourse on the nature of the self, using the story of King Suyajña and Yama's teaching to illustrate the illusion of attachment. The chapter ends with Diti and her daughters-in-law abandoning grief and focusing on truth.

story.title: Hiraṇyakaśipu's Grief and Philosophical Discourse
story.arc: Hiraṇyakaśipu, enraged by his brother's death, orders devastation; then consoles his family with teachings on the self.
story.timeline_note: After Hiraṇyākṣa's death; before Hiraṇyakaśipu's own death.
story.comic_potential: Hiraṇyakaśipu's dramatic rage and the vivid description of demonic destruction could be depicted with intense visuals.

teachings.teaching: The self (ātman) is eternal, unchanging, pure, all-pervading, and beyond the gunas; it only appears to be embodied due to māyā.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the true Self as distinct from the body-mind complex, achieved through sustained practice and discrimination.

teachings.teaching: Attachment to the body and its relationships is a delusion; the wise do not grieve for the dead.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga purifies the mind, enabling one to see the impermanence of the body and the permanence of the Self, thus transcending grief.

teachings.teaching: The story of the hunter and the birds illustrates how attachment leads to suffering and death.
teachings.lens_note: This parable underscores the Kriya-Yoga teaching that desire and attachment bind one to the cycle of karma; detachment is cultivated through inner discipline.

teachings.teaching: One should not grieve for the body, which is like a house made of elements, subject to change and destruction.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views the body as a temporary instrument for spiritual practice; identifying with it is a fundamental error corrected by meditation.

chapter_summary: Hiraṇyakaśipu performs severe penance to become immortal and invincible. The gods, distressed by his tapas, appeal to Brahmā, who grants Hiraṇyakaśipu a boon that makes him nearly impossible to kill, except under specific conditions.

story.title: Hiraṇyakaśipu's Boon
story.arc: Hiraṇyakaśipu performs extreme penance to gain immortality and invincibility. The gods, threatened, seek Brahmā's intervention. Brahmā appears and grants Hiraṇyakaśipu a boon that makes him invulnerable to most beings and circumstances, but with specific loopholes.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Hiraṇyakaśipu's brother Hiraṇyākṣa's death, before the Narasiṃha incarnation.
story.comic_potential: Hiraṇyakaśipu's extreme penance, with his body being eaten by ants, and his dramatic revival by Brahmā's water, has vivid visual and ironic elements.

teachings.teaching: The power of tapas (austerity) can compel even the gods to grant boons.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, tapas is the disciplined practice of inner fire, burning karmas and purifying the mind. Hiraṇyakaśipu's external penance symbolizes the intense inner effort required to transcend limitations.

teachings.teaching: Brahmā is the creator and bestower of boons, but even he operates within cosmic laws.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the divine responds to sincere practice, but the boon's conditions reflect the law of karma. Hiraṇyakaśipu's boon, with its loopholes, shows that even divine gifts are bound by dharma.

teachings.teaching: The description of Brahmā as the inner self and the source of all indicates the non-dual nature of reality.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees this as pointing to the Atman-Brahman identity. Hiraṇyakaśipu's praise of Brahmā as the inner controller reflects the yogic realization of the Self as the witness.

chapter_summary: Brahma grants boons to Hiranyakashipu after his severe penance. Empowered, Hiranyakashipu conquers all worlds and usurps Indra's throne, oppressing the universe. The gods seek refuge in Vishnu, who assures them that He will kill Hiranyakashipu when he persecutes his own son Prahlada, a great devotee. Prahlada's virtues and ecstatic love for Krishna are described.

story.title: Hiranyakashipu's Boon and Oppression; Prahlada's Devotion
story.arc: Hiranyakashipu receives boons from Brahma, conquers the universe, and persecutes his son Prahlada for his devotion to Vishnu.
story.timeline_note: After Hiranyakashipu's penance and boon, before his death at the hands of Narasimha.
story.comic_potential: Hiranyakashipu's opulent rule and Prahlada's ecstatic behavior provide vivid contrasts.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects His devotees and destroys the wicked in due time.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle that sustained practice (kriya) aligns one with divine will, ensuring protection and eventual dissolution of obstacles.

teachings.teaching: Hatred towards devotees, the Vedas, cows, brahmins, and the Lord leads to swift destruction.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that such hatred stems from ego (ahamkara), which kriya-yoga aims to dissolve through inner purification.

teachings.teaching: True devotion leads to detachment from material pleasures and ecstatic love for God.
teachings.lens_note: Prahlada's state exemplifies the pinnacle of kriya-yoga: constant absorption in the divine, transcending bodily consciousness.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the beginning of the Kurukshetra war, with Dhritarashtra asking Sanjaya about the activities of his sons and the Pandavas. Sanjaya narrates Duryodhana's address to Dronacharya, listing the prominent warriors on both sides, the blowing of conches, and the formation of armies.

story.title: The Gathering at Kurukshetra
story.arc: Beginning of the Kurukshetra War
story.timeline_note: First day of the war, just before the battle begins.
story.comic_potential: High drama with armies arrayed, conches blown, and heroes named.

teachings.teaching: The importance of righteous action on the sacred field of dharma.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the battlefield symbolizes the inner conflict between higher and lower tendencies; the 'dharmakshetra' is the purified mind ready for spiritual war.

teachings.teaching: The need to recognize and honor one's teachers and elders.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the guru's guidance is essential in navigating the inner battle; Duryodhana's approach to Dronacharya reflects the seeker's need to consult the inner teacher.

teachings.teaching: The distinction between material strength and spiritual protection.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets Bhishma's protection as the ego's defense, while Bhima's protection represents the inner strength of spiritual practice; the 'aparyaptam' and 'paryaptam' contrast the insufficiency of ego and sufficiency of divine will.

teachings.teaching: The power of divine sound (conch) to awaken spiritual energy.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the blowing of conches symbolizes the vibration of pranava (Om) that awakens the kundalini; Krishna's conch 'Panchajanya' represents the five elements purified.

chapter_summary: In this chapter, the sage Nārada recounts the story of Prahlāda and his father Hiraṇyakaśipu. The demon king Hiraṇyakaśipu, enraged by his son's devotion to Viṣṇu, attempts to kill Prahlāda through various means, but Prahlāda is protected by the Lord. The chapter explores themes of divine protection, the power of devotion, and the nature of true righteousness.

story.title: The Story of Prahlāda and Hiraṇyakaśipu
story.arc: Prahlāda's unwavering devotion to Viṣṇu despite his father's persecution, and the Lord's protection.
story.timeline_note: This is a past event narrated by Nārada to Yudhiṣṭhira.
story.comic_potential: High drama: a demon king's rage, a child's serene devotion, and divine interventions.

teachings.teaching: A father's anger towards a righteous son is unjust, as even ordinary parents correct children without malice.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this highlights the importance of non-attachment and equanimity in relationships, where discipline is given without ego or anger.

teachings.teaching: A devotee who is submissive, saintly, and respects guru and God should never be harmed.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the Kriya-Yoga principle of surrendering to the divine will, where the devotee's purity invokes divine protection.

chapter_summary: Hiraṇyakaśipu's son Prahlāda, despite being raised by demonic teachers, develops devotion to Viṣṇu. He teaches his father that true dharma is bhakti, not the three aims of life. Hiraṇyakaśipu tries to kill him through various means, but Prahlāda remains unharmed due to his devotion. The demon king becomes anxious, and his teachers advise binding Prahlāda until their return.

story.title: Prahlāda's Unwavering Devotion
story.arc: Prahlāda, despite being a demon's son, becomes a devotee of Viṣṇu and teaches his father about bhakti, leading to conflict.
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs during the childhood of Prahlāda, before the Narasiṃha episode.
story.comic_potential: High contrast between the demon king's rage and the serene child Prahlāda, with dramatic attempts on his life failing comically.

teachings.teaching: True dharma for embodied beings is to abandon the false identification with body and home, and take shelter of Hari.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This is the essence of Kriya-Yoga—turning inward and surrendering to the divine, cutting the root of worldly attachments.

teachings.teaching: The distinction between 'self' and 'other' is an illusion created by māyā; the true self is one.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The practice of Kriya-Yoga dissolves this duality, revealing the unity of ātman.

teachings.teaching: The nine forms of bhakti (śravaṇa, kīrtana, etc.) are the highest learning.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: These practices are the active limbs of Kriya-Yoga, engaging all senses in devotion.

teachings.teaching: Those who are attached to household life cannot develop devotion to Kṛṣṇa because their senses are uncontrolled.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Kriya-Yoga requires sense control; without it, one remains in the cycle of rebirth.

teachings.teaching: The purpose of human life is to attain Viṣṇu; those who seek external pleasures are like the blind leading the blind.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The goal of Kriya-Yoga is to realize the self as part of Viṣṇu, not to chase fleeting objects.

teachings.teaching: Devotion arises only when one takes shelter of the dust of the feet of great souls.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Association with realized beings (satsaṅga) is a key component of Kriya-Yoga practice.

chapter_summary: Prahlada instructs the Daitya children on the urgency of spiritual life, the futility of material pursuits, and the supremacy of devotion to Lord Vishnu. He emphasizes that human life is rare and should be used for attaining the Lord's lotus feet, not for transient pleasures. The chapter concludes with the Daitya children questioning how Prahlada, a child confined to the palace, gained such wisdom.

story.title: Prahlada's Instructions to the Daitya Children
story.arc: Prahlada, having been taught by Narada, imparts the knowledge of Bhāgavata-dharma to the young demons, urging them to abandon material attachments and take shelter of Lord Vishnu.
story.timeline_note: This occurs during Prahlada's childhood, after his education by Narada and before his confrontation with Hiranyakashipu.
story.comic_potential: The contrast between the demonic nature of the children and Prahlada's serene wisdom, along with their incredulous questions, offers dramatic and humorous moments.

teachings.teaching: One should begin practicing Bhāgavata-dharma from childhood, as human life is rare and fleeting.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the urgency of commencing spiritual practice (sādhana) early, before the body deteriorates.

teachings.teaching: Material pleasures are available everywhere, even in lower species; one should not waste life pursuing them.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a call to transcend sensory gratification (kriya) and focus on inner transformation.

teachings.teaching: The human life span is 100 years, but half is wasted in sleep and ignorance, and the rest in childhood play, old age, and sense gratification.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the need for disciplined time management in Kriya-Yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: Attachment to family and wealth binds one to material existence; it is difficult to break free.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as describing the obstacles (kleshas) that Kriya-Yoga aims to overcome.

teachings.teaching: One should cultivate compassion and friendship towards all beings, abandoning demoniac tendencies, to please the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on ethical conduct (yama) and devotion (bhakti).

teachings.teaching: The ultimate goal of dharma, artha, and kama is to surrender oneself to the Supreme Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the culmination of Kriya-Yoga: self-offering (ātma-nivedana).

teachings.teaching: Pure knowledge of devotion is rarely attained; it is taught by Narayana to Narada and is available to those who are detached and devoted.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the importance of guru-paramparā in Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: Narada narrates how Prahlada's mother was captured by Indra but rescued by Narada, and how Prahlada learned devotion from Narada while in the womb. Prahlada then teaches the Daitya children about the nature of the self, the futility of material pursuits, and the supreme importance of unalloyed devotion to Lord Vishnu.

story.title: Prahlada's Instruction to the Daitya Children
story.arc: Prahlada recounts his own past and imparts spiritual wisdom to the young demons.
story.timeline_note: Recounted by Narada to Yudhishthira; events occur before Prahlada's birth and during his childhood.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic rescue of pregnant mother, Indra's arrogance, Narada's intervention, Prahlada's profound teachings to bewildered demon children.

teachings.teaching: The self (ātman) is eternal, pure, and distinct from the body; one should give up the false identification with the body.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the initial stage of viveka (discrimination) where the practitioner distinguishes the seer from the seen.

teachings.teaching: The world is a combination of prakṛti (eightfold nature) and puruṣa; liberation comes through knowledge and devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes the practical realization of this through meditation on the puruṣa as distinct from prakṛti.

teachings.teaching: The three states of consciousness (waking, dream, deep sleep) are witnessed by the supreme self.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is realized through the practice of pratyahara and dharana, observing the witness consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The root cause of samsara is ignorance; the remedy is devotion to the Lord, which destroys the seeds of karma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga prescribes bhakti as the culmination of kriya, where surrender to the Lord purifies the heart.

teachings.teaching: Devotion is cultivated through service to the guru, association with devotees, hearing and chanting the Lord's glories, and meditation.
teachings.lens_note: These are the practical steps in Kriya-Yoga: guru-seva, satsanga, nama-japa, and dhyana.

teachings.teaching: Seeing the Lord in all beings and respecting them accordingly is a form of devotion.
teachings.lens_note: This is the practice of samadhi in daily life, recognizing the one consciousness in all.

teachings.teaching: The highest devotion leads to ecstatic symptoms like tears, trembling, and chanting, which indicate liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these are signs of the awakening of kundalini and the onset of bhava-samadhi.

teachings.teaching: Material wealth, family, and worldly achievements are temporary and cannot give lasting happiness; only devotion to the Lord yields eternal bliss.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches detachment (vairagya) as essential for progress, recognizing the impermanence of all material things.

teachings.teaching: The body and its possessions are not the self; one should seek the eternal Lord who is the self of all.
teachings.lens_note: This is the core of Kriya-Yoga: turning inward to realize the self as the Lord.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to the Lord is accessible to all, regardless of birth or status; even demons can attain liberation through pure bhakti.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga is universal, emphasizing that sincere practice, not external qualifications, leads to realization.

chapter_summary: Narada narrates the story of Hiranyakashipu's attempt to kill his son Prahlada for his devotion to Vishnu. Hiranyakashipu challenges Prahlada's assertion that Vishnu is everywhere, and strikes a pillar, from which Narasimha (half-man, half-lion) emerges and kills Hiranyakashipu. Various celestial beings then offer prayers to Narasimha.

story.title: The Slaying of Hiranyakashipu by Narasimha
story.arc: Prahlada's devotion is tested; Narasimha appears and kills the demon king; celestials offer prayers.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Prahlada's education by Narada; before the reign of Prahlada.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic confrontation, monstrous transformation, cosmic battle, and divine intervention.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is present everywhere, even in a pillar.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this signifies that the divine consciousness pervades all objects and can be realized through focused practice.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to God protects the devotee even from a powerful demon.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that unwavering devotion (ekantabhakti) aligns one's will with the divine, ensuring protection.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to protect the righteous and destroy the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the principle of divine intervention when cosmic balance is threatened.

chapter_summary: Prahlada, a devotee of Vishnu, is sent by the gods to pacify Narasimha after Hiranyakashipu's death. He offers a profound prayer, expressing humility, devotion, and philosophical insights about the nature of God and the path of bhakti. Narasimha, pleased, offers him a boon, but Prahlada refuses material desires, seeking only to serve the Lord.

story.title: Prahlada's Prayer and Narasimha's Grace
story.arc: After Narasimha kills Hiranyakashipu, the gods send Lakshmi and then Prahlada to pacify him. Prahlada offers a hymn of surrender and philosophical wisdom. Narasimha, pleased, offers a boon, but Prahlada refuses material desires, seeking only devotion.
story.timeline_note: Immediately after Narasimha's slaying of Hiranyakashipu.
story.comic_potential: The contrast between the fierce Narasimha and the calm, wise child Prahlada creates dramatic tension. Prahlada's refusal of boons despite divine pressure is humorous and poignant.

teachings.teaching: True devotion is superior to all material qualifications like birth, wealth, and learning.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that inner purification through bhakti is the essence, not external status.

teachings.teaching: God is not affected by our offerings; rather, worship benefits the worshipper.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework: the practice of devotion (kriya) transforms the practitioner, not the divine.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's incarnations are for the protection of the world and the destruction of evil.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma views avatars as cosmic kriyas, divine actions that restore balance.

teachings.teaching: The material world is a cycle of suffering; only surrender to God brings liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: the cycle of karma can be broken through disciplined practice and devotion.

teachings.teaching: The senses pull the mind in different directions; one must control them through devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: sensory control is a prerequisite for deeper meditation and union with the divine.

teachings.teaching: Compassion for those ignorant of God is a sign of true devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that a true yogi works for the welfare of others, not just personal liberation.

teachings.teaching: Material pleasures are trivial and cause more suffering; the wise seek higher bliss.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: the practice of yoga leads to inner bliss that surpasses sensory pleasures.

teachings.teaching: God is both immanent and transcendent; the wise see Him in all things.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework: the realized yogi perceives the divine presence in every aspect of existence.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Bhagavata Purana (Canto 7, Chapter 10) narrates the aftermath of Narasimha's killing of Hiranyakashipu. Prahlada refuses material boons from Lord Narasimha, emphasizing pure devotion without desire. The Lord instructs Prahlada to rule the demons and perform his father's funeral rites. Brahma praises the Lord, and the Lord explains that even enemies like Hiranyakashipu and Hiranyaksha, who were originally Vishnu's gatekeepers Jaya and Vijaya, attain liberation through constant enmity. The chapter concludes with the story of Jaya and Vijaya's three births as demons and their eventual return to Vishnu's abode.

story.title: Prahlada's Devotion and the Fate of Jaya and Vijaya
story.arc: After Narasimha kills Hiranyakashipu, Prahlada refuses boons, teaches pure devotion, and is appointed king of demons. The Lord explains the cycle of Jaya and Vijaya's births as demons and their liberation through enmity.
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs immediately after Narasimha's manifestation and killing of Hiranyakashipu, and includes a flashback to the original curse of Jaya and Vijaya and their subsequent births.
story.comic_potential: High. The dramatic dialogue between Prahlada and Narasimha, the cosmic revelation of Jaya and Vijaya's cycle, and the visual of Brahma's praise offer strong visual and narrative moments.

teachings.teaching: Pure devotion (bhakti) should be without any desire for material boons; a true servant does not seek rewards.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the principle of nishkama karma (desireless action) and the cultivation of bhakti as the highest yoga, where the practitioner offers all actions without attachment to fruits.

teachings.teaching: Even enemies of the Lord, if constantly absorbed in thought of Him, attain liberation and even union with Him.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes the power of constant remembrance (smarana) of the Divine, which can transform even negative emotions into a path to liberation, as seen in the case of Jaya and Vijaya.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's grace purifies even the most sinful, as seen in the purification of Hiranyakashipu through his son Prahlada's devotion.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the Kriya-Yoga concept of guru's grace (guru-kripa) and the transformative power of association with a pure devotee (satsanga).

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the aftermath of Prahlada's victory over Hiranyakashipu. Lord Narasimha, having killed the demon king, is pacified by Prahlada's prayers. The Lord then blesses Prahlada and establishes him as the ruler of the daityas. The chapter highlights the power of devotion and the Lord's mercy.

story.title: The Pacification of Lord Narasimha and the Blessing of Prahlada
story.arc: After the violent killing of Hiranyakashipu, Lord Narasimha's fierce anger is calmed by the prayers of Prahlada. The Lord then grants Prahlada rulership and assures him of protection.
story.timeline_note: This occurs immediately after the death of Hiranyakashipu, before Prahlada's coronation.
story.comic_potential: High contrast between the fierce Narasimha and the calm, devoted Prahlada; dramatic shift from rage to peace.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is easily pleased by pure devotion and can be pacified by the prayers of His devotees.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'bhakti yoga' where surrender and devotion transform the practitioner's inner state, calming the turbulent mind (symbolized by Narasimha's anger).

teachings.teaching: One who hears this narration with faith attains fearlessness and goes to the Lord's abode.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the power of 'sravana' (hearing) as a key practice in Kriya-Yoga, which purifies the mind and leads to liberation.

chapter_summary: Narada narrates to King Yudhishthira the story of Tripura-daha, where Lord Shiva, empowered by Vishnu's strategy, burns the three cities of the demons. The chapter highlights the supremacy of Vishnu and the interplay of divine will.

story.title: The Burning of Tripura
story.arc: Shiva destroys the three cities of the demons with Vishnu's help.
story.timeline_note: Past event narrated by Narada to Yudhishthira.
story.comic_potential: Vishnu and Brahma disguising as cow and calf to drink the nectar, and the demons' confusion.

teachings.teaching: No one is independent; all actions are controlled by divine destiny.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes surrender to the divine will and letting go of ego.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's pastimes purify the world.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees this as the power of divine stories to cleanse the mind.

chapter_summary: Nārada explains the eternal dharma for all varṇas and āśramas, as heard from Nārāyaṇa, detailing duties for brāhmaṇas, kṣatriyas, vaiśyas, śūdras, women, and mixed castes, emphasizing gradual detachment through prescribed actions.

story.title: The Eternal Dharma for All Varṇas
story.arc: Yudhiṣṭhira asks Nārada about sanātana dharma; Nārada explains duties based on varṇa and āśrama, culminating in the path of detachment.
story.timeline_note: After the story of Prahlāda and Vṛtrāsura, Yudhiṣṭhira questions Nārada.
story.comic_potential: A sage listing duties could be visually represented as a scroll or teaching scene.

teachings.teaching: The supreme dharma for all humans is devotion to Nārāyaṇa, consisting of thirty characteristics including hearing, chanting, remembering, serving, worshiping, bowing, servitude, friendship, and self-surrender.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these nine modes correspond to the nine limbs of bhakti-yoga, which are internal kriyas that purify the mind and lead to self-realization.

teachings.teaching: A brāhmaṇa's qualities include truthfulness, compassion, austerity, cleanliness, forbearance, introspection, control of mind and senses, non-violence, celibacy, renunciation, study, honesty, contentment, equanimity, service, detachment, silence, and self-analysis.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes these as kriyas that purify the sattva guna, preparing the practitioner for higher yoga.

teachings.teaching: A kṣatriya's qualities include heroism, valor, firmness, brilliance, charity, self-control, forgiveness, devotion to brāhmaṇas, compassion, and truthfulness.
teachings.lens_note: These are active kriyas that channel rajas towards dharma and service.

teachings.teaching: A vaiśya's qualities include devotion to gods and guru, pursuit of dharma-artha-kāma, faith, diligence, and skill in work.
teachings.lens_note: These kriyas harmonize rajas and tamas for worldly sustenance with spiritual orientation.

teachings.teaching: A śūdra's qualities include humility, cleanliness, service to the twice-born, and honesty.
teachings.lens_note: Service as a kriya purifies tamas and cultivates devotion.

teachings.teaching: A wife should serve her husband as a deity, with devotion, truthfulness, and love, and thereby attain the same world as Hari.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma sees this as a kriya of surrender, transforming household life into yoga.

teachings.teaching: By performing one's natural duties without attachment, one gradually becomes free from the modes of nature.
teachings.lens_note: This is the essence of karma-yoga as kriya: action without attachment purifies the mind.

teachings.teaching: Just as a field repeatedly sown loses fertility, the mind repeatedly indulging in desires becomes detached from them.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma uses this analogy to explain the kriya of repeated practice (abhyāsa) leading to vairāgya.

chapter_summary: Narada describes the duties and lifestyle of a brahmachari (celibate student) living in the guru's house, including daily routines, begging for alms, and celibacy rules. Then he outlines the vānaprastha (forest-dweller) stage, with its austerities, and finally the process of renunciation (sannyāsa) and meditation on the Supreme, culminating in liberation.

story.title: Duties of Brahmacarya and Vānaprastha
story.arc: Instruction on the stages of life (āśrama) leading to liberation
story.timeline_note: Timeless instruction; no narrative sequence.
story.comic_potential: Low; didactic text with no dramatic events.

teachings.teaching: A brahmacārī should live in the guru's house, serving him with humility and celibacy, and perform daily worship of guru, fire, sun, and deities.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes disciplined daily practice (kriya) and surrender to the guru as the external manifestation of the inner Self.

teachings.teaching: One should avoid association with women and sensual pleasures, as they agitate the mind even of a renunciate.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga stresses control of prana and senses; avoiding sensory triggers is essential for inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: After completing brahmacarya, one may enter household life, forest life, or renounce, but should always see the Supreme Lord present in all beings.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that the same consciousness pervades all; the practitioner should recognize the divine in every form.

teachings.teaching: A vānaprastha should live in the forest, eating wild foods, enduring heat and cold, and practicing austerities for a fixed period.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views such tapas as a means to purify the body and mind, preparing for higher meditation.

teachings.teaching: When the body becomes unfit, one should renounce by merging the elements and senses back into their cosmic sources, realizing the self as pure consciousness beyond duality.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga culminates in the dissolution of the ego into the Supreme, a process mirrored in the systematic withdrawal of prana and awareness.

chapter_summary: Narada describes the conduct of a paramahamsa sannyasi, who renounces all possessions and wanders alone, seeing the world as illusion. He recounts a dialogue between Prahlada and an avadhuta brahmana (the sage Ajagara), who teaches detachment through the example of a python and a bee.
