teachings.teaching: The highest devotion is characterized by intense longing and complete surrender to the divine, even in separation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this longing represents the soul's deep yearning for union with the Divine, a key aspect of bhakti yoga.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's pastimes and divine qualities are meditative focal points that purify the mind and grant liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that meditating on Krishna's lila (pastimes) is a powerful Kriya-Yoga technique to still the mind and realize the self.

teachings.teaching: The divine name and stories (katha) are life-giving nectar for the afflicted soul, removing sins and bestowing blessings.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's view that chanting and hearing divine narratives are essential Kriya-Yoga practices for spiritual purification.

teachings.teaching: True devotees transcend worldly attachments and social norms out of love for God.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga encourages transcending mundane identity through devotion, as exemplified by the Gopis leaving their families.

chapter_summary: The gopīs, overwhelmed by separation from Kṛṣṇa, sing and lament. Kṛṣṇa appears among them, and they joyfully welcome him. He takes them to the bank of the Yamunā, where they serve him. The gopīs question Kṛṣṇa about his apparent neglect of them, and he explains the nature of pure devotion, emphasizing that he reciprocates in a way that increases their attachment to him.

story.title: The Gopīs' Reunion with Kṛṣṇa
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's Vṛndāvana pastimes
story.timeline_note: After Kṛṣṇa's departure from the rāsa-līla, the gopīs lament and he returns to them.
story.comic_potential: The gopīs' dramatic mood swings from despair to joy, and their playful anger at Kṛṣṇa, offer comic relief.

teachings.teaching: Kṛṣṇa explains that he does not reciprocate equally with all devotees; he sometimes withholds his presence to increase their longing and devotion.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this teaching emphasizes that the divine may create obstacles or delays to deepen the practitioner's inner connection and selfless love, moving beyond transactional devotion.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama rescue Nanda from a serpent (a cursed Vidyadhara) and later kill the demon Shankhachuda, retrieving his jewel. The chapter highlights Krishna's divine power and protection of his devotees.

story.title: Krishna Rescues Nanda and Kills Shankhachuda
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Vrindavan
story.timeline_note: Occurs during Krishna's childhood in Vrindavan.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic rescue of Nanda from a serpent and the chase of Shankhachuda have visual and humorous elements.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's touch purifies even the most fallen, as seen when Krishna's foot touched the serpent and freed him from his curse.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that divine contact (satsanga) instantly removes karmic impurities and grants liberation.

teachings.teaching: The power of the Lord's name purifies both the speaker and the listener.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that chanting the divine name (nama-japa) is a key practice in Kriya-Yoga for inner purification.

teachings.teaching: Devotees should have faith in the Lord's protection, as Krishna assured the gopis 'Do not fear'.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of surrender (prapatti) to the divine will, leading to fearlessness.

chapter_summary: The Gopis describe Kṛṣṇa's flute playing and his pastimes in Vrindavan, expressing their intense longing and love for him. They narrate how all beings—animals, rivers, trees, and even gods—are captivated by his music.

story.title: The Gopis' Song of Kṛṣṇa's Flute
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's Vrindavan pastimes
story.timeline_note: During Kṛṣṇa's childhood in Vrindavan, after he goes to the forest with the cows.
story.comic_potential: The Gopis' exaggerated descriptions of nature's response to Kṛṣṇa's flute, such as rivers stopping and animals becoming statues, have a whimsical, almost humorous quality.

teachings.teaching: The power of divine sound (flute) to captivate all beings and draw them to the divine.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the flute represents the inner sound (nada) that awakens the soul's longing for union with the divine. The Gopis' absorption mirrors the yogi's state of samadhi.

teachings.teaching: Surrender and devotion (bhakti) as the highest path, exemplified by the Gopis and even animals and nature.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that true devotion involves complete surrender of the mind and senses, as seen in the Gopis' forgetfulness of worldly duties.

teachings.teaching: The divine is present in all of nature, and nature responds to the divine presence.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga view that the entire universe is a manifestation of consciousness, and when one attunes to that consciousness, nature itself becomes a teacher.

chapter_summary: Krishna kills the demon Arishta in the form of a bull, then Narada reveals to Kamsa that Krishna and Balarama are the sons of Vasudeva and Devaki, destined to kill him. Kamsa imprisons Vasudeva and Devaki, sends the demon Keshini to kill the boys, and plots their death through wrestlers and an elephant. He orders Akrura to bring Krishna and Balarama to Mathura for a bow sacrifice.

story.title: The Killing of Arishta and Kamsa's Plot
story.arc: Krishna's childhood pastimes in Vrindavan and the lead-up to Mathura
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the killing of Putana and other demons, before the Mathura episode.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic bull-demon attack, Narada's revelation, Kamsa's rage, and the plotting of multiple schemes.

teachings.teaching: The divine protects the innocent and destroys the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the inner purification where the practitioner's divine will (Krishna) overcomes the demonic tendencies (Arishta) that disrupt spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: One should not be attached to the results of actions; success and failure depend on destiny.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the practitioner should perform actions with detachment, surrendering the outcome to the divine, as Akrura advises Kamsa.

teachings.teaching: The wise see the hand of fate in all endeavors.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle of accepting divine will and not being swayed by success or failure.

chapter_summary: Krishna kills the demon Keshī in horse form, then kills the demon Vyoma who kidnapped cowherd boys. The sage Nārada visits Krishna, praises him, and prophesies his future deeds.

story.title: Krishna Slays the Demons Keshī and Vyoma
story.arc: Krishna's childhood pastimes in Vraja
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's return to Vraja and before the killing of Kaṃsa.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic horse-demon attack, Krishna's playful hiding game with cowherd boys, and the giant demon Vyoma kidnapping boys.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the supreme self, the witness, and the controller of all beings.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this affirms that the inner self (ātman) is identical to Krishna, the witness consciousness that pervades all.

teachings.teaching: Krishna creates the universe through his māyā and then enters it as the inner controller.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the yogi must transcend māyā by realizing the self as the witness, not the doer.

teachings.teaching: Krishna descends to protect the righteous and destroy the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view that divine incarnation is a teaching to inspire spiritual practice and devotion.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is pure consciousness, free from the flow of māyā's guṇas.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this describes the state of the realized yogi who abides in pure awareness beyond the three guṇas.

chapter_summary: Akrūra travels from Mathurā to Gokula, meditating on Kṛṣṇa's glories and his own good fortune. Upon arrival, he sees the footprints of Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, then meets them personally. He prostrates, is embraced by Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, and is honored with hospitality by Nanda and the brothers.

story.title: Akrūra's Journey to Gokula
story.arc: Akrūra travels to Gokula, meditates on Kṛṣṇa, meets Him and Balarāma, and is honored.
story.timeline_note: After Kaṃsa's invitation to the wrestling match; before the wrestling arena events.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's internal monologue of ecstatic devotion while traveling, contrasting with his official mission.

teachings.teaching: Seeing the Lord is extremely rare and is the result of great merit.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the rare attainment of samādhi through sustained practice.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's feet are worshipped by great beings and are the refuge for the fearful.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes surrendering the ego at the Lord's feet as the culmination of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial but reciprocates with devotees according to their devotion.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle that the divine responds to the intensity of one's practice.

teachings.teaching: The purpose of human life is to give up pretense, fear, and sorrow by seeing, hearing, and touching the Lord's symbols.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is the process of internalizing divine qualities through meditation on the Lord's form.

chapter_summary: Akrura arrives in Vrindavan to take Krishna and Balarama to Mathura for the festival. The gopis lament their impending separation from Krishna. Krishna and Balarama depart, and the gopis are left in grief. On the way, Akrura bathes in the Yamuna and has a vision of the cosmic form of Vishnu and the divine serpent Ananta Shesha.

story.title: Akrura's Arrival and the Gopis' Lament
story.arc: Krishna's departure from Vrindavan to Mathura
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Putana and other demons, Akrura comes to take Krishna and Balarama to Mathura for the bow festival.
story.comic_potential: The gopis' dramatic lament and Akrura's underwater vision of Vishnu provide vivid imagery.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' intense separation from Krishna illustrates the pain of divine love and the soul's longing for God.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this separation represents the initial stage of bhakti where the practitioner feels intense longing for the divine, which purifies the heart.

teachings.teaching: Akrura's vision of Vishnu and Ananta Shesha in the Yamuna reveals the omnipresence of the divine.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as the inner vision achieved through purification and meditation, where the practitioner sees the cosmic form within.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's assurance to the gopis that he will return teaches that divine separation is temporary and leads to reunion.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the cycle of practice: the devotee experiences separation (viraha) but is assured of the divine's return through continued sadhana.

chapter_summary: Akrūra offers a profound prayer to Kṛṣṇa, acknowledging Him as the supreme cause of all causes, the primordial Puruṣa. He describes how all elements, senses, and deities emanate from Him, yet they cannot comprehend His true nature. Akrūra praises various avatāras of the Lord, including Matsya, Hayagrīva, Kūrma, Varāha, Narasiṃha, Vāmana, Paraśurāma, Rāma, Vāsudeva, Saṅkarṣaṇa, Pradyumna, Aniruddha, Buddha, and Kalki. He laments his own ignorance and bondage to material existence, seeking refuge at Kṛṣṇa's lotus feet.

story.title: Akrūra's Prayer to Kṛṣṇa
story.arc: Akrūra, having been sent by Kaṃsa to bring Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma to Mathurā, offers a heartfelt prayer acknowledging Kṛṣṇa's supremacy and seeking His mercy.
story.timeline_note: This prayer occurs during Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Vṛndāvana, just before He leaves for Mathurā.
story.comic_potential: The prayer is a monologue; visual potential lies in the descriptions of the avatāras and the cosmic form.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate cause of all causes, and all elements, senses, and deities are His parts.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization that the entire universe is a manifestation of the Supreme Consciousness, and all actions are to be offered to that source.

teachings.teaching: The material elements and even the demigods cannot comprehend the Lord's true nature.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the limitation of intellectual knowledge; Kriya-Yoga emphasizes direct experience through meditation.

teachings.teaching: Different paths of worship (Vedic rituals, yoga, jñāna, etc.) all ultimately lead to the same Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga synthesizes these paths, focusing on inner purification and union with the divine.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's avatāras descend to protect the righteous and destroy the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: Each avatāra represents a specific divine intervention; in Kriya-Yoga, they are seen as archetypes for inner transformation.

teachings.teaching: The conditioned soul is bewildered by māyā and identifies with the body and possessions.
teachings.lens_note: This is the root of suffering; Kriya-Yoga aims to break this identification through disciplined practice and surrender.

teachings.teaching: Surrender to the Lord is the only means to overcome material bondage.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, surrender (prapatti) is the culmination of practice, leading to liberation.

chapter_summary: Akrūra sees the divine vision of Kṛṣṇa in the Yamunā, then returns to Mathurā with Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma. They enter the city, are welcomed by the citizens, and Kṛṣṇa kills the washerman, blesses the weaver, and grants boons to the garland-maker Sudāmā.

story.title: Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma Enter Mathurā
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's journey to Mathurā and his initial encounters
story.timeline_note: After the vision in the Yamunā, Akrūra returns with Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma to Mathurā at sunset.
story.comic_potential: The washerman's arrogant refusal and swift decapitation, the weaver's joyful service, and the garland-maker's humble devotion provide vivid, contrasting scenes.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's form is both immanent and transcendent; He reveals and withdraws His divine form at will.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of pratyāhāra: the withdrawal of the senses from external objects to perceive the inner Self.

teachings.teaching: True devotees see the Lord everywhere and in all elements.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the yogi perceives the divine consciousness pervading all creation, as Akrūra states.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is equal to all beings and bestows grace according to one's devotion.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga teaching of samatva (equanimity) and the law of karma: the Lord responds to the devotee's inner attitude.

teachings.teaching: The highest boon is unflinching devotion to the Lord, friendship with His devotees, and compassion for all beings.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga considers bhakti (devotion) as the culmination of yogic practice, leading to liberation.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama enter Mathura, encounter the hunchbacked woman Trivakra who offers them sandalwood paste, and Krishna straightens her body. They then break the great bow in the arena, kill Kamsa's guards, and spend the night in the city. Kamsa, terrified by omens, arranges a wrestling match the next day. The chapter ends with the wrestlers and Nanda's party arriving.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama in Mathura
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Mathura leading to the slaying of Kamsa
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna and Balarama leave Vrindavan and enter Mathura; the wrestling match is set for the next day.
story.comic_potential: The straightening of Trivakra's hunchback and the breaking of the bow are visually striking scenes.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's touch can transform and elevate even the most humble and deformed.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the transformative power of divine grace (kripa) that straightens the crooked mind and aligns it with the true self.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is not bound by material laws; He acts with playful spontaneity (lila).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Krishna's breaking of the bow effortlessly demonstrates the yogic principle of acting without attachment, as a master of karma.

teachings.teaching: Fear of death arises from ignorance of the self; Kamsa's omens reflect his inner turmoil.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets Kamsa's nightmares as the mind's projection of its own fears, which can be overcome through meditation and self-knowledge.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama, having heard the wrestling arena's drum, go to see it. At the entrance, Krishna confronts and kills the elephant Kuvalayapida, then enters the arena with Balarama. The people praise them, recalling their past deeds. Canura challenges them to wrestle, and Krishna agrees.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama Enter the Arena
story.arc: Kamsa's downfall
story.timeline_note: After the killing of various demons in Vrindavan, Krishna and Balarama go to Mathura for the wrestling festival.
story.comic_potential: High: elephant fight, crowd reactions, dramatic entry.

teachings.teaching: The divine protects the righteous and destroys the wicked, as Krishna effortlessly kills the elephant and demons.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the power of divine will (iccha-shakti) when aligned with dharma; action without attachment yields effortless victory.

teachings.teaching: One should act in accordance with time and place (desha-kala-ucita), as Krishna responds appropriately to Canura's challenge.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga practice must adapt to circumstances; the wise act harmoniously with the present context.

teachings.teaching: The king represents all beings (sarva-bhuta-maya), so pleasing him brings universal benefit.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the principle of seeing the divine in all; service to the embodied divine (king) is service to all beings.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama defeat the wrestlers Canura, Mustika, Kuta, Sala, and Tosala in the Mathura arena, then Krishna kills Kamsa. The women of Kamsa's family lament, and Krishna and Balarama release their parents Devaki and Vasudeva from bondage.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama Kill Kamsa's Wrestlers and Kamsa
story.arc: Kamsa's downfall and the liberation of Devaki and Vasudeva
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs after Krishna and Balarama have arrived in Mathura for the wrestling match arranged by Kamsa.
story.comic_potential: High; the wrestling match and the dramatic killing of Kamsa on the dais are visually striking and action-packed.

teachings.teaching: The divine play of the Lord is beyond ordinary comprehension; even the women spectators recognize the injustice of the match and praise Krishna and Balarama.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'sakshi-bhava' (witness consciousness) where the onlookers discern the higher purpose behind apparent conflict.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate protector and destroyer; those who oppose him face destruction, while his devotees are liberated.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the 'kriya' of surrender to the divine will leads to liberation, as seen when Devaki and Vasudeva are freed.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's actions are always for the welfare of the world; even violence is justified when it removes adharma.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the concept of 'dharma-yuddha' (righteous action) where the yogi acts without attachment to results.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama return to Mathura, reunite with their parents Vasudeva and Devaki, and console them. They install Ugrasena as king, welcome back the Yadava clans who had fled due to Kamsa, and send Nanda and the gopas back to Vrindavan. They then undergo the sacred thread ceremony with Garga as teacher and study under Sandipani in Avanti, learning all scriptures and arts in 64 days. To fulfill the guru's request for dakshina, they retrieve the guru's son from the ocean, killing the demon Panchajana, and bring the boy back from Yama's city.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama's Return to Mathura and Education
story.arc: Krishna's early life and education
story.timeline_note: After Kamsa's death, Krishna and Balarama return to Mathura, reunite with parents, install Ugrasena, then undergo upanayana and study under Sandipani.
story.comic_potential: The scene where Krishna and Balarama, as young boys, master all knowledge in 64 days and then retrieve the guru's son from the ocean and Yama's realm has high comic potential, especially the casual way they handle divine tasks.

teachings.teaching: One must serve parents and elders; failing to do so leads to suffering after death.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that service to parents is a form of karma yoga that purifies the heart and prepares one for higher practices.

teachings.teaching: A guru's request must be fulfilled even at great personal cost.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, guru-seva is paramount; the guru is the conduit to the divine, and fulfilling his wish accelerates spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's lila as a human conceals his divinity, teaching humility.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma notes that Krishna's 'māyā-manuṣya' form demonstrates that even the divine submits to human relationships, teaching the aspirant to see the divine in all.

chapter_summary: Uddhava is sent by Krishna to Vrindavan to console Nanda, Yashoda, and the gopis who are suffering from separation. Uddhava delivers Krishna's message, describing his divine nature and assuring them of his return. The chapter ends with the gopis' morning activities and their curiosity about Uddhava's arrival.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to Vrindavan
story.arc: Krishna's separation and Uddhava's consolation
story.timeline_note: After Krishna killed Kamsa and settled in Mathura, he sends Uddhava to Vrindavan.
story.comic_potential: The emotional reunion and the gopis' curiosity about Uddhava's identity.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial and equal to all; he has no favorites or enemies.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the state of samatvam (equanimity) where the yogi sees the same consciousness in all beings.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's actions are for the protection of the righteous and for his own pleasure, not bound by karma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the realized soul acts without personal motive, like Krishna's lila.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the inner self of all beings, the ultimate cause and substratum.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga teaching of realizing the Atman as identical with Brahman.

chapter_summary: Uddhava visits Vrindavan as Krishna's messenger. The gopis express their intense separation from Krishna, recounting their love and pain. Uddhava delivers Krishna's message of spiritual union through constant remembrance. The gopis respond with both joy and longing. Uddhava stays for some months, consoling them with Krishna's pastimes, then returns to Mathura.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to Vrindavan
story.arc: Krishna's separation and the gopis' devotion
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura, before his return to Vrindavan.
story.comic_potential: The gopis' dramatic lamentations and Uddhava's consoling message create a poignant contrast.

teachings.teaching: The gopis describe worldly relationships as self-interested and temporary, contrasting with their selfless love for Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the need to transcend transactional relationships and cultivate unconditional devotion (bhakti) as the highest yoga.

teachings.teaching: Krishna teaches that he is ever-present within all beings and elements, and that separation is an illusion meant to deepen love.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the apparent separation of the individual self from the divine is a play of māyā; constant remembrance (smaraṇa) bridges the gap.

teachings.teaching: Uddhava praises the gopis' devotion, stating that it is superior to all other spiritual practices.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga holds that spontaneous love for the divine (prema-bhakti) is the culmination of all yogic paths, surpassing mere ritual or knowledge.

teachings.teaching: The gopis express that even though they know detachment is the highest happiness, their hope for Krishna's return remains unshakable.
teachings.lens_note: This paradox of knowing and yet hoping reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle that the soul's innate longing for the divine cannot be extinguished by intellectual understanding.

teachings.teaching: Uddhava concludes that the gopis' devotion is so powerful that even the dust of their feet purifies the three worlds.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees such devotion as the ultimate transformative force, capable of purifying all existence.

chapter_summary: Krishna visits the courtesan's house, fulfills her desire, then goes to Akrura's house. Akrura praises Krishna and is sent to Hastinapura to check on the Pandavas.

story.title: Krishna's Visit to Akrura and the Mission to Hastinapura
story.arc: Krishna fulfills the courtesan's desire, then visits Akrura, who praises him. Krishna sends Akrura to Hastinapura to check on the Pandavas.
story.timeline_note: After Kamsa's death, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's awe and Krishna's playful responses could be humorous.

teachings.teaching: The Lord fulfills the desires of his devotees, even material ones, to eventually lead them to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates how the Guru uses desires as a means to draw the disciple towards higher consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is beyond material qualities and actions, unaffected by the modes of nature.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the true Self (Atman) is untouched by prakriti, similar to Krishna's transcendence.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to protect dharma and destroy the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view that divine descent (avatara) occurs to re-establish spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Saints purify by their mere sight, unlike holy waters or deities made of material elements.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the Guru's presence is considered more purifying than external rituals.

chapter_summary: Akrūra visits Hastinapura, meets with Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Bhīṣma, Vidura, Kuntī, and others. Kuntī laments her sons' exile and prays to Kṛṣṇa. Akrūra advises Dhṛtarāṣṭra to treat the Pāṇḍavas equally, but Dhṛtarāṣṭra admits his attachment to his sons. Akrūra returns to Dvārakā and reports to Balarāma and Kṛṣṇa.

story.title: Akrūra's Mission to Hastinapura
story.arc: Pre-Kurukshetra diplomacy and Kuntī's lament
story.timeline_note: After the Pāṇḍavas' exile, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's diplomatic visit, Kuntī's emotional outburst, Dhṛtarāṣṭra's inner conflict.

teachings.teaching: One is born alone, dies alone, and experiences the results of one's actions alone.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the solitary nature of spiritual practice; each individual must perform their own sadhana.

teachings.teaching: Wealth acquired unrighteously is taken away by others; one who accumulates it ignorantly is abandoned by wealth, life, and family, and falls into darkness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a warning against attachment to material gains, which distract from the inner yogic path.

teachings.teaching: See this world as a dream, illusion, or mental construct; control the mind and become equal and peaceful.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on inner detachment and meditation to realize the transient nature of the world.

teachings.teaching: No one can change the will of the Lord, who descends to relieve the earth's burden.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as acknowledging the divine plan; the yogi surrenders to the cosmic will.

chapter_summary: Jarāsandha, king of Magadha, attacks Mathurā with a vast army to avenge his sons-in-law Kaṃsa's death. Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, recognizing the divine purpose to relieve the earth's burden, engage in battle. Kṛṣṇa destroys the army but spares Jarāsandha, who is captured by Balarāma and then released. Jarāsandha attacks seventeen more times, each time defeated. On the eighteenth, the Yavana king Kālayavana arrives, prompting Kṛṣṇa to build a fortress city in the sea (Dvārakā) to protect the Yadus. The chapter ends with Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma leaving Mathurā to confront Kālayavana.

story.title: Jarāsandha's Attacks and the Building of Dvārakā
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes as a king and protector of the Yadus
story.timeline_note: After Kaṃsa's death, Jarāsandha attacks Mathurā multiple times; the eighteenth attack is imminent when Kālayavana appears.
story.comic_potential: Kṛṣṇa's playful sparing of Jarāsandha despite his repeated defeats, and the dramatic construction of Dvārakā.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to relieve the earth's burden and protect the righteous.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of dharma-rakṣā: the divine descends to restore balance when adharma prevails, and the practitioner must similarly act to uphold righteousness in their own life.

teachings.teaching: True heroes do not boast but demonstrate their prowess through action.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga is a path of practice, not mere talk; one must show spiritual progress through disciplined action.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's actions, though seemingly human, are divine play (līlā) and beyond comprehension.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view that the realized soul acts without attachment, seeing all as the Lord's play.

chapter_summary: Mucukunda, a king from the Ikshvaku dynasty, had helped the gods by guarding their realm. After being granted a boon, he chose to sleep in a cave. Later, the Yavana king pursued Krishna into the cave, where Mucukunda was sleeping. The Yavana kicked Mucukunda, who then burned him with his angry glance. Krishna then revealed himself to Mucukunda, who offered prayers and sought liberation. Krishna granted him unwavering devotion and a future birth as a brahmana.

story.title: Mucukunda's Liberation
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes with Mucukunda
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's killing of Kamsa and other demons, during his earthly pastimes.
story.comic_potential: The Yavana's mistaken kick and Mucukunda's sleepy anger leading to instant incineration has humorous potential.

teachings.teaching: The material world is full of suffering; one should seek refuge in the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this echoes the need to turn inward and practice kriya to overcome material bondage.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to the Lord is superior to all boons and material achievements.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that kriya-yoga leads to unwavering devotion, which is the highest goal.

teachings.teaching: The body is temporary; one should not be attached to it.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-yoga teaches detachment from the body through pranayama and meditation.

teachings.teaching: Association with saints leads to devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma stresses the importance of satsanga in the path of kriya-yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the events leading to Krishna's marriage with Rukmini. After the demon Mura's defeat, Krishna returns to Dvaraka, defeats Jarāsandha's army, and later marries Rukmini, the princess of Vidarbha, by abducting her in the Rākṣasa style after defeating her brother Rukmi and other kings. The chapter includes Rukmini's message to Krishna expressing her love and her plan for him to abduct her.

story.title: Krishna's Marriage to Rukmini
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After the defeat of Mura and before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic abduction, battle scenes, romantic tension, and divine intervention.

teachings.teaching: Contentment is the greatest wealth; even a poor contented man sleeps happily, while a discontented king suffers.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, contentment (santoṣa) is a key niyama that purifies the mind and allows one to perceive the self. This verse emphasizes that inner peace is independent of external possessions.

teachings.teaching: A brāhmaṇa who is content with whatever comes, without deviating from his dharma, fulfills all desires.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle of svādhyāya (self-study) and īśvara-praṇidhāna (surrender to God), where one's duty is performed without attachment to results.

teachings.teaching: The wise bow to brāhmaṇas who are content, compassionate, egoless, and peaceful.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary highlights humility and service to the wise as a means to purify the heart and advance in yoga.

teachings.teaching: A king who protects his subjects and ensures their happiness is dear to the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga ideal of karma-yoga: performing one's duty selflessly for the welfare of others.

teachings.teaching: Rukmini's devotion: She considers Krishna as her only refuge and is willing to give up her life if she does not attain his grace.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's framework, this exemplifies bhakti-yoga as the highest path, where the devotee surrenders completely to the divine will.

chapter_summary: Krishna, having received Rukmini's message, decides to abduct her before her marriage to Shishupala. He travels to Kundina with his charioteer Daruka. Meanwhile, King Bhishmaka prepares for the wedding, and many enemy kings assemble. Rukmini anxiously awaits Krishna. A brahmana messenger confirms Krishna's arrival. Rukmini goes to the temple of Ambika (Parvati) to pray, then exits. As she boards her chariot, Krishna abducts her in the presence of all the kings, who are stunned. Balarama and the Yadava army support Krishna.

story.title: The Abduction of Rukmini
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmini
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Rukmini sends a message to Krishna (bhp_10.52) and before the wedding battle.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic abduction in full view of the assembled kings, who are stunned and drop their weapons, has high comic potential.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's response to Rukmini's love shows that divine love is reciprocal and that the Lord responds to the devotee's longing.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'kriya' as dynamic interaction: the devotee's intense longing (kriya) draws the divine response, manifesting as grace.

teachings.teaching: Rukmini's prayer to Ambika (Parvati) for a husband like Krishna shows the importance of seeking divine blessings for a righteous marriage.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that all actions, including marriage, should be consecrated to the divine. Rukmini's prayer is a model of 'kriya' as offering one's will to God.

teachings.teaching: The kings' infatuation with Rukmini's beauty and their subsequent defeat illustrate the power of maya and the futility of material desire.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is a cautionary tale: attachment to sense objects (maya) leads to downfall. The kings' loss of composure symbolizes the mind's entanglement in illusion.

chapter_summary: Krishna abducts Rukmini from her svayamvara, defeats the assembled kings including Shishupala and Rukmi, spares Rukmi's life at Rukmini's request, and marries her in Dvaraka with great celebration.

story.title: The Abduction of Rukmini
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmini
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's victory over Kamsa and before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Rukmi's disfigurement and his vow not to enter Kundina until he kills Krishna, leading to his self-imposed exile.

teachings.teaching: The body is subject to birth and death, but the self is eternal and unaffected.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This teaching emphasizes the distinction between the eternal self and the temporary body, a core principle for transcending identification with the body.

teachings.teaching: One should not grieve over the inevitable; the wise understand the transient nature of worldly affairs.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This aligns with the practice of equanimity (samatva) in Kriya-Yoga, where the yogi remains undisturbed by dualities.

teachings.teaching: The self is one in all beings; differences arise from ignorance.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This teaching supports the realization of unity consciousness, a goal of advanced Kriya-Yoga practice.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of Pradyumna, the son of Krishna and Rukmini, who is an incarnation of Kamadeva. As an infant, he is stolen by the demon Shambara and thrown into the ocean, where a fish swallows him. The fish is caught and brought to Shambara's kitchen, where the child is discovered by Mayavati (Rati in disguise). Raised by her, Pradyumna grows up, learns his true identity from Rati, and defeats Shambara in battle, cutting off his head. He then returns to Dvaraka, where he is joyfully reunited with his family.

story.title: The Return of Pradyumna
story.arc: Pradyumna's abduction, growth in the demon's house, slaying of Shambara, and reunion with family in Dvaraka.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's marriage to Rukmini and before the later pastimes in Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: High: infant swallowed by fish, raised by demon's cook, identity reveal, dramatic battle, and joyful homecoming.

teachings.teaching: The divine plan unfolds even through apparent tragedies; the demon's attempt to kill the child leads to his growth and eventual victory.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates how obstacles (kleshas) are transformed into opportunities for spiritual growth when one surrenders to the divine will.

teachings.teaching: True identity is revealed through divine grace and the guidance of a guru (Narada).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the role of the guru in awakening the disciple to their true nature as an eternal part of the divine.

teachings.teaching: The power of divine knowledge (vidya) overcomes all illusions (maya).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that the practice of meditation and mantra (vidya) destroys the veils of maya, leading to self-realization.

teachings.teaching: The reunion of the soul with the divine is a cause for supreme joy.
teachings.lens_note: This mirrors the Kriya-Yoga goal of reuniting the individual soul (jiva) with the Supreme Soul (Paramatma) through disciplined practice.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of the Syamantaka jewel. Satrajit, a devotee of the Sun god, receives the jewel from Surya. His brother Prasena is killed by a lion while wearing it, and the lion is killed by Jambavan. Krishna is falsely accused of taking the jewel. To clear his name, Krishna tracks the jewel to Jambavan's cave, fights him for 28 days, and ultimately reveals his divine identity. Jambavan offers his daughter Jambavati and the jewel to Krishna. Krishna returns the jewel to Satrajit, who, repentant, gives his daughter Satyabhama and the jewel to Krishna. Krishna accepts Satyabhama but declines the jewel.

story.title: The Syamantaka Jewel
story.arc: Krishna's divine glories and clearing of false accusations
story.timeline_note: Occurs during Krishna's residence in Dvaraka, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High: mistaken identity, a 28-day fight with a bear, a jewel that produces gold, and a dramatic courtroom-style clearing of Krishna's name.

teachings.teaching: The Lord clears the false accusations against him, showing that divine reputation is ultimately protected.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'karma-shuddhi' – purification of actions and their consequences through surrender to the divine will.

teachings.teaching: Jambavan's recognition of Krishna as the Supreme Lord after a fierce battle demonstrates that even the most powerful beings must acknowledge the divine.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga concept of 'ishvara-pranidhana' – surrender to the Lord as the ultimate goal of all spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: Satrajit's repentance and offering of his daughter and jewel show that material attachments must be surrendered to attain peace.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the necessity of 'vairagya' (detachment) and 'bhakti' (devotion) to overcome the bondage of karma.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama visit Hastinapura after the Kuru fire incident, then return to Dvaraka. Satrajit is murdered by Shatadhanva for the Syamantaka jewel. Shatadhanva flees, pursued by Krishna and Balarama. Krishna kills Shatadhanva but the jewel is not found. Balarama goes to Mithila, Krishna returns to Dvaraka. Later, Krishna retrieves the jewel from Akrura, clearing his name.

story.title: The Syamantaka Jewel: Murder and Redemption
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After the Kuru fire incident, before the Mahabharata war; Duryodhana learns mace-fighting from Balarama during this period.
story.comic_potential: High: chase scene, jewel theft, mistaken accusations, dramatic reveal.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's actions are beyond human comprehension; even great sages are bewildered by His maya.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This illustrates the principle of surrendering the intellect (buddhi) to the divine will, recognizing that the ego's attempts to understand the absolute are futile.

teachings.teaching: One should not be attached to material wealth; the Syamantaka jewel brought only trouble.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Attachment to objects (including the 'jewel' of the body) leads to suffering; the practice of vairagya (detachment) is essential for inner peace.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects the righteous and punishes the wicked, but His mercy is impartial.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The divine acts through the law of karma; the practitioner must cultivate equanimity, seeing all events as the Lord's grace.

chapter_summary: Krishna visits the Pandavas in Indraprastha, marries Kalindi (daughter of Surya), and later marries Satya (daughter of Nagnajit) after subduing seven bulls. He also marries Bhadra, Lakshmana, and many other wives rescued from the demon Bhauma.

story.title: Krishna's Marriages and the Pandava Connection
story.arc: Krishna visits Indraprastha, marries Kalindi, then Satya after subduing bulls, and later marries Bhadra, Lakshmana, and rescues many wives from Bhauma.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the Pandavas' establishment in Indraprastha, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's playful subduing of seven bulls and Arjuna's hunting expedition provide light-hearted moments.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's visit to the Pandavas demonstrates the importance of maintaining family bonds and friendship.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of satsanga (association with the divine) as a means to purify the mind.

teachings.teaching: Kunti's expression of gratitude to Krishna for sending her brother (Vasudeva) highlights the importance of remembering the divine in times of distress.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes constant remembrance (smarana) of the divine as a means to overcome suffering.

teachings.teaching: Kalindi's penance and devotion to Vishnu as her desired husband illustrates the power of single-minded devotion.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this exemplifies the practice of ishta-devata (chosen deity) meditation, focusing all energies on one goal.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's subduing of the seven bulls without effort shows that divine grace overcomes all obstacles.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that through surrender to the divine, one can transcend all limitations (kleshas).

teachings.teaching: The king Nagnajit's condition of subduing the bulls before marriage symbolizes the need to conquer one's own senses before attaining the divine.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the seven bulls represent the seven chakras or the five senses plus mind and intellect; conquering them is essential for spiritual progress.

chapter_summary: Krishna, accompanied by his wife Satyabhama, travels to the city of Pragjyotisha to kill the demon Narakasura. After defeating Naraka and his forces, Krishna recovers the earrings of Aditi, frees 16,000 captive princesses, and returns to Dvaraka. He then marries the princesses and later uproots the Parijata tree from Indra's heaven for Satyabhama.

story.title: The Slaying of Narakasura and the Rescue of the Princesses
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's marriage to Satyabhama and before the Parijata episode.
story.comic_potential: High: The battle scenes with Mura and Naraka, the dramatic rescue of the princesses, and the comedic uprooting of the Parijata tree for a jealous wife.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's power is absolute; he effortlessly defeats demons and protects devotees.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the power of divine grace (kripa) that removes obstacles on the path of yoga.

teachings.teaching: Surrender to the Lord (prapatti) is the ultimate refuge; even the demon's mother Bhumi seeks Krishna's protection for her son.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that surrender (prapatti) is the essence of bhakti-yoga, leading to liberation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial and fulfills the desires of his devotees, as seen in the Parijata episode.
teachings.lens_note: This shows that the Lord responds to the pure love of his devotees, a key principle in Kriya-Yoga where devotion (bhakti) is the means to union.

chapter_summary: Krishna playfully tests Rukmini's devotion by pretending to be unworthy of her, causing her distress. She responds with a heartfelt discourse on her unwavering love and surrender, leading to Krishna's reassurance and their loving reconciliation.

story.title: The Playful Test of Rukmini's Devotion
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini, during their early married life in Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: High: Krishna's teasing and Rukmini's dramatic distress, followed by her eloquent rebuttal and their tender reconciliation, offer rich emotional and humorous interplay.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's apparent indifference or testing is a form of loving play that deepens devotion.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this mirrors the guru's testing of the disciple's sincerity; the disciple's unwavering faith purifies the heart and leads to deeper union.

teachings.teaching: True love for God is unconditional, not based on material qualities or benefits.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga cultivates desireless devotion (ahaituki bhakti), where the practitioner loves God for His own sake, not for rewards.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the sole refuge and fulfiller of all desires; material kings and pleasures are transient.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga teaching of turning inward to the Self (Atman) as the source of all fulfillment, rather than seeking external objects.

teachings.teaching: A woman who does not relish the nectar of the Lord's feet is deluded by the body, which is a corpse-like collection of impurities.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary often highlights the need to transcend bodily identification through Kriya-Yoga practices that reveal the soul's eternal nature.

teachings.teaching: The highest gain in household life is loving conversation with one's beloved, which mirrors the soul's communion with the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the householder path is sanctified when all actions are offered to the Divine, and relationships become a means of spiritual growth.

teachings.teaching: Those who worship the Lord for material gains are deluded by Maya; the true devotee seeks only the Lord, who is the embodiment of liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that Kriya-Yoga purifies the mind of material desires, leading to the realization that God alone is the supreme goal.

chapter_summary: This chapter lists the sons of Krishna by his chief queens and describes the marriage of Aniruddha to Rocana, the dice game between Balarama and Rukmi, and the subsequent killing of Rukmi by Balarama.

story.title: The Sons of Krishna and the Marriage of Aniruddha
story.arc: Genealogy and the dice game incident
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini and other queens, their sons are listed. Then the story moves to Aniruddha's marriage to Rocana, which leads to the dice game and Rukmi's death.
story.comic_potential: The dice game and Balarama's rage, including knocking out Kaliṅga's teeth, have high comic potential.

teachings.teaching: The queens of Krishna, despite being sixteen thousand, could not conquer his mind with their charms, showing his supreme control.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the state of a yogi who remains unaffected by sensory distractions, maintaining inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: Even though Rukmi was insulted by Krishna, he gave his daughter in marriage to Aniruddha out of affection for his sister, showing the power of family bonds.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle of acting without attachment to ego, prioritizing dharma and relationships over personal grudges.

teachings.teaching: Balarama's anger and subsequent killing of Rukmi demonstrates that even great souls may act in wrath when provoked, but the divine plan prevails.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's lens sees this as a reminder that emotions like anger can be transformed into righteous action when aligned with cosmic order, but one must be cautious of karmic consequences.

chapter_summary: King Parikshit asks Shuka about the marriage of Aniruddha to Usha, daughter of Bana, and the ensuing battle between Krishna and Shiva. Shuka narrates: Bana, a devotee of Shiva, had a thousand arms and became arrogant. Shiva prophesied his flag would fall when he fought an equal. Bana's daughter Usha dreamt of Aniruddha and fell in love. Her friend Citralekha, a yogini, identified Aniruddha and brought him to Usha. They lived together secretly. Bana discovered them and captured Aniruddha with serpent ropes. Usha lamented.

story.title: The Abduction of Aniruddha and the Battle with Bana
story.arc: Parikshit asks about the marriage of Aniruddha to Usha and the battle between Krishna and Shiva. Shuka narrates Bana's boon, Usha's dream, Citralekha's help, the secret union, discovery, and capture of Aniruddha.
story.timeline_note: This story occurs after Krishna's incarnation, during the time of the Yadava dynasty.
story.comic_potential: High: romantic dream, secret love, magical transport, dramatic confrontation, and a cliffhanger with Aniruddha bound.

teachings.teaching: Pride leads to downfall: Bana's arrogance about his thousand arms prompts Shiva's prophecy of his defeat.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, pride (ahamkara) is a major obstacle; it must be dissolved through surrender to the divine will.

teachings.teaching: Divine love transcends barriers: Usha's love for Aniruddha, whom she saw in a dream, leads to union despite physical separation.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the power of bhakti (devotion) which, in Kriya-Yoga, is a means to unite the individual soul with the Supreme.

teachings.teaching: The power of yoga: Citralekha uses her yogic powers to transport Aniruddha across distances.
teachings.lens_note: Yogic siddhis (perfections) are byproducts of Kriya-Yoga practice, but should not be the goal; they can be used for divine purposes.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama lead the Vrishnis to rescue Aniruddha from Bana's fortress. A fierce battle ensues between Krishna and Shiva, and between their respective armies. Krishna defeats Shiva's forces, cuts off Bana's arms except four, and spares him due to Prahlada's boon. Bana surrenders, returns Aniruddha and Usha, and the Vrishnis return home.

story.title: The Rescue of Aniruddha and the Battle with Bana
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Aniruddha's capture by Bana; four months pass before the Vrishnis learn of it.
story.comic_potential: High: cosmic battle with gods, demons, and divine weapons; humorous moment with Kotara appearing naked; Jvara's fever duel.

teachings.teaching: The supreme Lord is the source of all, and all beings are manifestations of His energy.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization that the individual self is not separate from the cosmic Self; the practice of Kriya-Yoga dissolves the illusion of duality.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to protect dharma and relieve the earth's burden.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that divine incarnations demonstrate the path of righteous action (dharma) and the importance of surrendering to the divine will.

teachings.teaching: One who remembers the divine pastimes of the Lord is freed from fear and defeat.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, constant remembrance (smriti) of the divine is a key practice to transcend the ego and attain inner peace.

teachings.teaching: Material existence is like a fever; surrender to the Lord brings relief.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets the fever as the burning of karmic impressions; Kriya-Yoga purifies the subtle body, leading to liberation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the inner controller and the ultimate refuge for all beings.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga goal of realizing the indwelling presence (Atman) as the guide.

chapter_summary: King Nṛga, a former king of the Ikṣvāku dynasty, recounts his fall from grace due to an inadvertent misdeed involving a Brahmin's cow. After giving away a cow that had strayed into his herd, two Brahmins claimed it, leading to a dispute. Unable to resolve it, Nṛga died and was turned into a lizard (kṛkalāsa) in a dry well. Lord Kṛṣṇa rescues him, restoring his divine form, and Nṛga praises Kṛṣṇa before ascending to heaven. Kṛṣṇa then teaches the Yadus about the grave sin of misappropriating Brahmin property.

story.title: The Deliverance of King Nṛga
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: Occurs during Kṛṣṇa's residence in Dvārakā, after the defeat of Bāṇāsura (Chapter 63).
story.comic_potential: High: A dramatic rescue of a giant lizard from a well, followed by a celestial ascent and a moral lesson about Brahmin property.

teachings.teaching: Misappropriation of Brahmin property, even unknowingly, leads to severe karmic consequences.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this underscores the principle of karma and the importance of mindful action (kriya) to avoid unintended sin. The story illustrates how even a king's inadvertent mistake can lead to downfall, emphasizing the need for constant awareness and alignment with dharma.

teachings.teaching: One should never harm a Brahmin, even if they are at fault; instead, bow to them always.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework highlights humility and surrender (praṇāma) as a Kriya-Yoga practice to purify the ego and align with the divine will. This teaching reinforces the need to transcend personal grievances through devotion.

teachings.teaching: The sin of stealing Brahmin property is worse than poison and destroys the thief's family for generations.
teachings.lens_note: This emphasizes the law of cause and effect (karma) in Kriya-Yoga, where actions have far-reaching consequences. It serves as a warning to rulers and householders to respect the property of those dedicated to spiritual life.

chapter_summary: Balarama visits Nanda's Gokula, is welcomed by the gopas and gopis who inquire about Krishna. He consoles them with Krishna's messages, stays for two months, and enjoys pastimes with the gopis. He summons the Yamuna river, which initially disobeys, but then submits; Balarama drags her with his plow, then releases her. He sports in the water and is adorned by the river goddess.

story.title: Balarama's Visit to Vraja and the Yamuna's Submission
story.arc: Balarama's pastimes in Vraja
story.timeline_note: After Krishna and Balarama's return to Mathura, Balarama visits Gokula for two months during the spring season (Madhu and Madhava months).
story.comic_potential: Balarama's drunken state, dragging the Yamuna with his plow, and the river's plea create a humorous and dramatic scene.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' intense love for Krishna and their feeling of separation (viraha) is a form of devotion.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this viraha is the intense longing that purifies the heart and leads to union with the divine.

teachings.teaching: Balarama's anger and subsequent mercy towards Yamuna illustrates that even divine beings must respect the Lord's authority, and that surrender leads to grace.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that ego (represented by Yamuna's initial disobedience) must be humbled before the divine will; surrender brings liberation.

chapter_summary: Paundraka, a foolish king, claims to be Vasudeva and sends a messenger to Krishna demanding he surrender. Krishna kills Paundraka and his ally the king of Kashi. The king's son Sudakshina performs a ritual to invoke a fiery demon to avenge his father, but Krishna's Sudarshana chakra destroys the demon and burns Varanasi.

story.title: The Killing of Paundraka and the Burning of Varanasi
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as protector and destroyer of the wicked
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Balarama's visit to Nanda's Vraja (bhp_10.65).
story.comic_potential: High: Paundraka's absurd imitation of Krishna, the fiery demon, and the chakra's rampage offer vivid visual scenes.

teachings.teaching: False pride and imitation of the divine lead to destruction.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the danger of ego (ahamkara) masquerading as spiritual attainment. True realization requires surrender, not imitation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects his devotees and destroys the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that divine grace (kriya) acts as both shield and sword, responding to the devotee's call.

teachings.teaching: Rituals performed with wrong intent (abhicara) backfire on the performer.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma notes that kriya-yoga purifies intention; impure actions yield negative results, as seen when the demon burns Sudakshina himself.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about Balarāma's extraordinary deeds. Śuka narrates how the demon Dvivida, a friend of Naraka and a minister of Sugrīva, wreaks havoc across the land to avenge his friend's death. He eventually confronts Balarāma on Raivataka mountain, where Balarāma is drinking and singing with women. Dvivida mocks and harasses them, leading to a fierce battle. Balarāma kills Dvivida with his mace, and the gods praise him.

story.title: The Slaying of Dvivida
story.arc: Balarāma's pastimes
story.timeline_note: After Naraka's death, Dvivida seeks revenge.
story.comic_potential: Dvivida's antics, including showing his rear to the women, and Balarāma's drunken state provide humorous elements.

teachings.teaching: The demon Dvivida, though powerful, is ultimately defeated by the divine will of Balarāma.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that even the most obstinate karmic obstacles (dvivida = 'twofold' ignorance) are dissolved when one surrenders to the divine (Balarāma = strength of spiritual practice). The battle represents the inner struggle against duality.

chapter_summary: Samba, son of Krishna and Jambavati, abducts Lakshmana, daughter of Duryodhana, from her svayamvara. The Kauravas capture him. Balarama goes to Hastinapura to negotiate but is insulted. He then uproots the city with his plow and threatens to throw it into the Ganges. The Kauravas surrender and offer gifts. Balarama returns to Dvaraka.

story.title: The Abduction of Lakshmana and Balarama's Wrath
story.arc: Krishna's Pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the svayamvara of Lakshmana, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Balarama's plow dragging the city of Hastinapura into the Ganges is a visually striking and humorous scene, especially the panic of the Kauravas.

teachings.teaching: The power of a devotee (Balarama) is supreme; even kings must bow before divine will.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of surrender (prapatti) to the divine, which dissolves ego and brings protection.

teachings.teaching: Pride leads to downfall; the Kauravas' arrogance caused their humiliation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that ego (ahankara) is the root of suffering; humility is essential for spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: Forgiveness and mercy are shown even to enemies who surrender.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga teaching of compassion (daya) as a natural expression of inner purity.

chapter_summary: Narada visits Dvaraka to see how Krishna simultaneously lives with his 16,108 wives. He witnesses Krishna in each palace, engaged in different activities, demonstrating his yoga-maya. Narada praises Krishna and departs, and the chapter concludes with the glory of hearing this pastime.

story.title: Narada's Visit to Krishna's Palaces
story.arc: Krishna's divine pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Narada hears of Krishna's marriage to 16,108 wives and the slaying of Narakasura.
story.comic_potential: Narada's bewildered expression as he sees Krishna in multiple places at once, doing different activities.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's simultaneous presence in all palaces demonstrates his yoga-maya and divine opulence.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of simultaneous action (saha-kriya) where the divine can perform multiple actions without division, reflecting the unity of consciousness.

teachings.teaching: Narada's humility and devotion: he bows to Krishna and considers himself blessed.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that true knowledge leads to humility and service; Narada exemplifies the ideal disciple.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's statement: 'I am the speaker, doer, and approver of dharma; I teach by example.'
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on living dharma through action (kriya) and not just theory.

teachings.teaching: Hearing and chanting Krishna's pastimes leads to devotion and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's path includes kirtan and sravanam as essential practices for purifying the mind.

chapter_summary: Krishna's daily routine in Dwaraka, including his morning activities, worship, and court session. A messenger from imprisoned kings arrives seeking help against Jarāsandha. Nārada appears and informs Krishna about Yudhiṣṭhira's plan to perform the Rājasūya sacrifice. Krishna consults Uddhava.

story.title: Krishna's Daily Routine and the Imprisoned Kings
story.arc: Preparation for Rājasūya
story.timeline_note: Morning in Dwaraka, before the Rājasūya sacrifice
story.comic_potential: Krishna's divine routine contrasted with mundane activities; Nārada's sudden appearance; the messenger's dramatic plea.

teachings.teaching: The importance of daily spiritual practice (sandhyā, japa, dāna) even for an avatar.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This illustrates the necessity of disciplined practice (kriya) for all, as even Krishna performs rituals to set an example.

teachings.teaching: The world is like a dream; true happiness is in the self, not in external objects.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This echoes the teaching that liberation comes from turning inward and realizing the self as distinct from the body and mind.

teachings.teaching: Hearing, chanting, and meditating on the Lord purify even the lowest.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: These are key practices (kriya) that lead to purification and ultimately to realization.

chapter_summary: Uddhava advises Krishna to kill Jarāsandha to enable the Rājasūya sacrifice, and Krishna agrees. The Yādava army travels to Indraprastha, where Yudhiṣṭhira and the Pāṇḍavas welcome them. Krishna stays for several months, enjoying pastimes with Arjuna.

story.title: Uddhava's Counsel and Krishna's Journey to Indraprastha
story.arc: Preparation for Rājasūya
story.timeline_note: After Nārada's visit, Uddhava speaks; Krishna then travels to Indraprastha and stays for several months.
story.comic_potential: The grand procession of the Yādava army and the emotional reunion of Krishna with the Pāṇḍavas.

teachings.teaching: Killing Jarāsandha is necessary for the Rājasūya sacrifice and for the welfare of many.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the necessity of removing obstacles (karmic impurities) through decisive action (kriya) to achieve higher spiritual goals.

teachings.teaching: One should not refuse a request from brāhmaṇas.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle of humility and service to the wise, which purifies the mind.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's pastimes are sung by devotees for liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, singing or meditating on the Lord's acts is a form of bhakti that leads to inner stillness and self-realization.

chapter_summary: Yudhiṣṭhira expresses his desire to perform the Rājasūya sacrifice. Kṛṣṇa advises him to first conquer all kings. Yudhiṣṭhira sends his brothers to subdue the directions. They bring tribute, but Jarāsandha remains unsubdued. Kṛṣṇa, Bhīma, and Arjuna, disguised as brāhmaṇas, go to Girivraja. They challenge Jarāsandha to a duel. Bhīma fights Jarāsandha and, with Kṛṣṇa's hint, splits his body in two. Kṛṣṇa installs Jarāsandha's son Sahadeva as king and releases the imprisoned kings.

story.title: The Killing of Jarāsandha
story.arc: Preparation for the Rājasūya sacrifice
story.timeline_note: After the Pāṇḍavas' exile and before the great war.
story.comic_potential: High: Disguised heroes, a boastful villain, a dramatic duel with a clever hint, and a gory but victorious split.

teachings.teaching: One should perform righteous acts like the Rājasūya sacrifice to attain glory and please the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the principle of 'kriya' or purposeful action aligned with dharma, which purifies the mind and leads to self-realization.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial and bestows grace according to one's devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the Lord's grace is like a wish-fulfilling tree, responding to the intensity of one's practice (sadhana).

teachings.teaching: Even an unstable body can be used to attain eternal fame by serving the wise and performing noble deeds.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga teaching that the body is a temporary instrument for spiritual evolution; one should use it for selfless service and yoga practice.

teachings.teaching: A true king should be generous and protect brahminical values, even at the cost of his life.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework highlights 'daiva' (divine will) and 'purushartha' (human effort); here, Jarāsandha's initial respect for brahmins contrasts with his later pride, showing the need for balanced effort.

chapter_summary: Krishna releases 20,800 kings imprisoned by Jarasandha, who praise Krishna and renounce worldly desires. Krishna instructs them on devotion and righteous rule, then honors them and sends them back. He then has Bhima kill Jarasandha and returns to Indraprastha with Arjuna and Sahadeva, where Yudhishthira rejoices.

story.title: Release of the Imprisoned Kings
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka and Indraprastha
story.timeline_note: After Jarasandha's defeat, before the Rajasuya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic release of 20,800 kings from a mountain cave, their awe at seeing Krishna, and the subsequent battle with Jarasandha.

teachings.teaching: Wealth and power lead to pride and downfall; one should seek refuge in Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the purification of the ego (ahamkara) through surrender to the divine will, leading to inner peace.

teachings.teaching: The material world is like a mirage; only the Supreme is real.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes discrimination (viveka) between the eternal and the temporary, a key step in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Krishna is the ultimate goal, not material enjoyment.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga path of bhakti as the culmination of yogic practices.

teachings.teaching: One should perform duties without attachment, fixing the mind on Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: This is the essence of karma-yoga in Kriya-Yoga: action without attachment to results.

chapter_summary: Yudhishthira performs the Rajasuya sacrifice. During the ceremony, Sahadeva proposes that Krishna be given the highest honor. Shishupala objects and insults Krishna, leading to Krishna killing Shishupala with his discus. The sacrifice concludes successfully.

story.title: The Rajasuya Sacrifice and the Death of Shishupala
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as the supreme lord
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Jarasandha (previous chapter), Yudhishthira performs the Rajasuya. Shishupala's death occurs during the ceremony.
story.comic_potential: High drama: Sahadeva's proposal, Shishupala's outburst, Krishna's silent response, and the sudden decapitation.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the supreme, non-dual reality, the source of all beings and sacrifices.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this affirms that the ultimate goal of all practices is to realize the oneness of the self with Krishna, the inner controller.

teachings.teaching: One should not tolerate blasphemy of the Lord or his devotees; those who hear it and do not leave are fallen.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the mind must be protected from negative influences; hearing criticism of the divine weakens one's spiritual resolve.

teachings.teaching: Constant enmity and meditation on the Lord, even with hatred, can lead to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that focused attention, even in negative emotions, can purify the mind if directed towards the divine, as seen in Shishupala's merging with Krishna.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the Rājasūya sacrifice of King Yudhiṣṭhira, the distribution of duties among his brothers and relatives, the grand celebration including music, dance, and bathing in the Gaṅgā, the departure of guests, and Duryodhana's envy and humiliation when he mistakes water for solid ground in the magical assembly hall, leading to his anger and departure.

story.title: The Rājasūya Sacrifice and Duryodhana's Humiliation
story.arc: Yudhiṣṭhira's rise to emperor and the seeds of the Kurukṣetra war
story.timeline_note: After the Rājasūya sacrifice, before the dice game and exile.
story.comic_potential: Duryodhana's mistaken step into water, thinking it solid ground, and Bhīma's laughter provide a moment of dark comedy.

teachings.teaching: The Rājasūya sacrifice demonstrates the ideal of righteous kingship and the importance of honoring all beings.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of 'dharma' as the foundation of action, where the king's duty is to perform sacrifices for the welfare of all, aligning individual will with cosmic order.

teachings.teaching: Duryodhana's envy and humiliation illustrate the destructive nature of pride and attachment.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a warning against 'ahamkara' (ego) and 'mamata' (mine-ness), which bind the soul to suffering. The incident shows how misperception (māyā) leads to downfall.

teachings.teaching: The bathing in the Gaṅgā symbolizes purification from sins.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this represents the cleansing of karmic impressions through the practice of 'prāṇāyāma' and meditation, leading to inner purity.

chapter_summary: King Śālva, a friend of Śiśupāla, vows to destroy the Yadus after being defeated at Rukmiṇī's wedding. He propitiates Lord Śiva and obtains a flying city called Saubha, made by Maya Dānava. He attacks Dvārakā, causing havoc. Pradyumna leads the Yādava defense, countering Śālva's illusions and fighting fiercely. Pradyumna is struck down by Śālva's minister Dyumān but is rescued by his charioteer, who explains he acted out of duty to protect his master.

story.title: The Slaying of Śālva
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: After Rukmiṇī's wedding, before the Mahābhārata war.
story.comic_potential: The flying city Saubha and its illusions provide spectacular visual effects; the duel between Pradyumna and Dyumān is intense; the charioteer's moral dilemma adds drama.

teachings.teaching: A warrior should never retreat from battle; it is a disgrace to one's family.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of steadfastness in one's dharma, akin to unwavering focus in meditation.

teachings.teaching: A charioteer's duty is to protect his master, even if it means retreating temporarily.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the importance of discernment (viveka) in action, a key aspect of Kriya-Yoga practice.

chapter_summary: Krishna battles the Saubha king Shalva, who uses illusion to deceive Krishna into thinking his father Vasudeva has been captured and killed. Krishna overcomes the illusion, destroys the Saubha flying city, and kills Shalva. The chapter also mentions the aftermath of the Rajasuya sacrifice and Shishupala's death.

story.title: The Slaying of Shalva
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes as a warrior king
story.timeline_note: After the Rajasuya sacrifice and Shishupala's death, Krishna returns to Dvaraka and then fights Shalva.
story.comic_potential: High; dramatic battle with flying city, illusion of father's death, and beheading of Shalva.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is unaffected by illusion and material emotions like grief and delusion.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the state of a yogi who remains established in the self (sva-bodha) even when confronted with apparent calamities, recognizing them as māyā.

teachings.teaching: One should not boast but demonstrate prowess through action.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that true spiritual strength is shown through disciplined practice (kriya) rather than mere words.

chapter_summary: Krishna kills Dantavakra and his brother Viduratha, then returns to Dvaraka. Meanwhile, Balarama, to avoid taking sides in the Kuru-Pandava war, goes on a pilgrimage. At Naimisha Forest, he kills the disrespectful suta Romaharshana (Lomaharshana) with a blade of grass, incurring brahmana-hatya. The sages prescribe a penance: kill the demon Balvala and then perform a twelve-month pilgrimage.

story.title: The Death of Dantavakra and Balarama's Pilgrimage
story.arc: Post-Sisupala and Salva battles; pre-Kurukshetra war
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the deaths of Sisupala, Salva, and Paundraka; before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High: Krishna's effortless killing of Dantavakra and Viduratha, Balarama's pilgrimage with encounters, and the dramatic killing of Romaharshana with a blade of grass.

teachings.teaching: The Lord kills those who are envious of Him and His devotees, even if they are relatives.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this represents the destruction of ego and negative tendencies that arise from the mind (Krishna's relatives).

teachings.teaching: A true sage should be humble and respectful; pride in learning without self-control is useless.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga practice must be accompanied by humility and discipline; mere intellectual knowledge is barren.

teachings.teaching: The Lord takes avatars to punish the hypocrites who wear the garb of dharma.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is the inner purification where the divine will removes false pretenses in the practitioner.

teachings.teaching: Even a great sin like brahmana-hatya can be atoned for by serving the sages and performing pilgrimage.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views pilgrimage as a metaphor for inner journey; the prescribed penance symbolizes systematic purification of the subtle body.

chapter_summary: Balarama travels to various holy places, kills the demon Balvala, witnesses the Kurukshetra war, attempts to stop the duel between Bhima and Duryodhana, returns to Dvaraka, and later performs a sacrifice at Naimisha forest, teaching pure knowledge to the sages.

story.title: Balarama's Pilgrimage and Intervention at Kurukshetra
story.arc: Balarama's travels, killing of Balvala, pilgrimage to holy sites, witnessing the Kurukshetra war, attempting to stop the duel, returning to Dvaraka, and teaching at Naimisha.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the killing of Balvala and before the end of the Kurukshetra war; Balarama's pilgrimage spans many holy sites.
story.comic_potential: Balarama's dramatic entry at the battlefield, his attempt to stop the duel, and the sages' adoration after the sacrifice.

teachings.teaching: Balarama taught the sages pure knowledge (vishuddha vijnana) by which one realizes the self and the universe as pervaded by the Supreme.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the inner Self (Atman) as identical with Brahman, achieved through disciplined practice and purification.

teachings.teaching: One who remembers Balarama's deeds morning and evening becomes dear to Vishnu.
teachings.lens_note: This emphasizes the practice of constant remembrance (smarana) as a key Kriya-Yoga technique for attaining divine grace.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about the glories of Kṛṣṇa. Śuka narrates the story of a poor brāhmaṇa, a former classmate of Kṛṣṇa, who is urged by his wife to seek help from Kṛṣṇa. The brāhmaṇa goes to Dvārakā, is warmly received by Kṛṣṇa, and they reminisce about their time at the guru's hermitage. Kṛṣṇa emphasizes the importance of guru-sevā and the brāhmaṇa's devotion.

story.title: The Brāhmaṇa and the Lord
story.arc: Devotion and Guru-sevā
